Configuring the BIG-IP System to Auto-Populate Pools
Using host names to identify pool members and nodes
You create nodes on the BIG-IP system to represent the backend servers on your network. In turn, you create pool members to represent the backend servers on your network when you create a pool and want to load balance traffic to multiple backend servers.
You can configure a BIG-IP system with nodes and pool members that are identified with fully-qualified domain names (FQDNs). When you configure pool members with FQDN, addresses will dynamically follow DNS changes. Fully dynamic DNS-managed pools may even be created. Overview: Diameter

The Diameter protocol provides message-routing functionality that the BIG-IP system supports in a load-balancing

Diameter message
        routing configuration
In a message routing configuration, the

        Diameter routing configuration with requests from Client 1 and Client 2 to servers located
        in different destination realms, Realm-A and Realm-B.
A Diameter
          message routing configuration
A typical Diameter message routing configuration with two realms
        involves configuring the following items.
Functionality
Description
Pool
A pool for each realm directs Diameter traffic to
                servers.
Session profile
A session profile for each realm configures a session as

results.content:
# Article K01052782

Topic
The BIG-IP system provides several Ratio load balancing methods that you can select for load balancing traffic to pool
members. When you configure a ratio load balancing method, you assign a ratio weight to each pool member.
Description
Ratio
method Description When to use
Ratio
(member)
The Ratio (member) method can be used to load balance most
connection types. When you configure the Ratio (member) load
balancing method you should assign a ratio weight to each pool
member that is proportional to the capacity of the server. The number
of connections that each server receives over time is proportionate to
the assigned ratio weight. Ratio (member) load balancing calculations
are localized to each specific pool (member-based calculation), as
opposed to the Ratio (node) method in which calculations are applied
to all pools of which the node is a member.
You can use the Ratio
(member) load balancing
method when the server
capacity of the pool members
is different and you want to
assign user-specified ratio
weights that are proportional
to the resources of each pool
member.
Ratio (node)
The Ratio (node) method can be used to load balance most
connection types. When you configure the Ratio (node) load
balancing method, the number of connections that each server
receives over time is proportionate to a ratio weight you assign to
each node. Ratio (node) load balancing calculations are applied to all
The Ratio (node) method can
be used when the server
capaci

results.title: Bug ID 651108: Only Round Robin load balancing is supported with Application Connector nodes
results.content:
F5 Bug ID 651108: Only Round Robin load balancing is supported with Application Connector nodes

Severity: 3-Major | Opened: Mar 13, 2017 | Fixed In: 1.2.0 | Affected Versions: 1.1.0, 1.1.1

Symptoms: When you add cloud nodes provided by Application Connector to a pool using any load balancing algorithm other than Round Robin, load balancing fails and all traffic is directed to the first pool member in the list. This may also either prevent traffic from going to the cloud nodes , or force 100% of the traffic to the cloud nodes, if the cloud pool members co-exist with local pool members in the pool.

Conditions: Cloud nodes provided by Application Connector exist in a pool, and the pool's load balancing method is not Round Robin.

Impact: Since cloud pool members from Application Connector have no pool member statistics, all of the statistical load balancing methods will fail because the stats will read 0. For example if the load balancing method is Least Connections, the first pool member will always have 0 connections associated with it so all traffic will go to the first pool member.

Workaround: Always use Round Robin load balancing with cloud nodes provided by Application Connector.

Related articles: K10134038

Data scraped: 2026-06-27

results.title: K42275060: There are several Load Balancing Methods. Which one is
results.content:
# Article K42275060

Description
You are trying to determine which BIG-IP LTM load balancing method will work best for your application and you want to
know which one to choose. The factors to consider are the pool members and the ability of each one to handle the load
directed to it and the network. There are many load balancing methods and some methods have various sub-methods.
By default the BIG-IP will use Round Robin which will work fine for most deployments.
Environment
Each environment has its own particulars, but at a basic level the following will apply:
BIG-IP LTM
The virtual server carrying the traffic, pool members, and the load balancing method
The products, settings and features vary, but will revolve around the needs of the application and the network
environment running the traffic.
With that in mind here is an overview of each method:
Round Robin: The system passes each new connection request to the next server in line, eventually distributing
connections evenly across the array of machines being load balanced. This method works well in most
configurations, especially if the equipment that you are load balancing is roughly equal in processing speed and
memory.
Ratio (member): The number of connections that each machine receives over time is proportionate to a ratio
weight you define for each machine within the pool.
Least Connections (member): The system passes a new connection to the node that has the least number of
current connections in the pool. This met

results.title: K02024845: BIG-IP LTM load balancing | BIG-IP LTM-DNS operations
results.content:
# Article K02024845

Chapter 2: BIG-IP LTM load balancing
Table of contents |  | << Previous chapter Next chapter >>
BIG-IP systems are designed to distribute client connections to load balancing pools, which are typically made up of
multiple pool members. The selected load balancing method (or algorithm) determines how connections are distributed
across pool members.
Your choice of load balancing method may depend on factors such as pool member availability or session affinity:
Some load balancing methods distribute connections evenly across pool members, regardless of the current
workload of pool members or nodes. Such methods work best with homogeneous server pools and workloads.
Some load balancing methods favor higher performing servers. These methods may result in deliberately uneven
traffic distribution across pool members.
Some load balancing methods consider factors which change at run time, such as current connection count or
capacity.
Contents
Chapter sections
Load balancing types
Static load balancing methods
Dynamic load balancing methods
Priority Group Activation
Monitors
Monitor benefits

results.title: K000147622: What are the different DNS load-balancing methods and
results.content:
# Article K000147622

Description
I want to know what the different DNS load-balancing methods are and how they work.
Environment
F5® Distributed Cloud (SaaS)
F5 Distributed Cloud DNS Load Balancer
Cause
None
Answer/Recommended Actions
The Ratio-Member load balancing method
When you configure a pool to use the Ratio load balancing method, the DNS Load Balancer distributes requests across
the pool members based on their assigned weights. The system calculates the weight of each pool member as a
percentage of the total weight of all pool members. This percentage determines how frequently each pool member
receives connection requests.
Consider the fictional company SiteRequest, where the wide IP www.siterequest.com contains a pool named poolMain.
This pool contains three members, with the following weight assignments:
Virtual Server 1: weight 50
Virtual Server 2: weight 25
Virtual Server 3: weight 25
Each time DNS Load Balancer selects this pool, it balances the load across all three members. Over time, the load
balancing statistics for this pool appear as follows:
Virtual Server 1: selected 50 percent of the time
Virtual Server 2: selected 25 percent of the time
Virtual Server 3: selected 25 percent of the time
If Virtual Server (VS)1 goes down: then we're left with VS2 and VS3. Since they have the same ratio each (25), the load
balancing applied will be 50% to VS1 and 50% to VS2.
K000147622: What are the different DNS load-balancing methods and
how do they work?

results.title: K82433558: Quick deployment: BIG-IP DNS Round Robin load
results.content:
# Article K82433558

Topic
This article guides you through a quick BIG-IP DNS Round Robin deployment. There are many more BIG-IP DNS
configuration options available to customize to the needs of your network and applications, and these are detailed further
in the documents linked in the section of this article.Supplemental Information 
You should consider using this procedure under the following conditions:
You want to provision and deploy a BIG-IP DNS system in your environment.
You want to configure BIG-IP DNS Round Robin load balancing.
Description
The BIG-IP DNS system performs global server load balancing (GSLB), whereby the system distributes name resolution
requests to data centers in various geographical locations using a defined load balancing method. This article describes
how to provision the BIG-IP DNS module and configure Round Robin load balancing between two data centers.
The Round Robin load balancing algorithm distributes DNS name resolution requests in a sequential pattern among the
virtual servers in a pool or among pools in a multiple pool configuration. Over time, each virtual server receives
approximately the same number of requests. Using the Round Robin load balancing is useful when you want to
distribute requests equally among all resources.
Round Robin load balancing example
The web site www.example.com is hosted in two geographically separate data centers, Data Center1 - West Coast and
Data Center2 - East Coast. In this example, BIG-IP DNS provides G

Description
You notice that the pool members for a pool is unable to load balance connections evenly
 Environment
Round Robin Load Balancing Method
Multiple Pool Members
No Persistence Profile
No One connect profile
No iRule configured
 Cause
Instead of using Current Connection to determine if the Pool is load balancing evenly you should
calculate the Delta of Total Connection
 Recommended Actions
You can view the number of total connections for each pool member in the GUI which can be helpful in
determining the delta value.
To get the difference of the Total connection do the following:
Go to Local Traffic > Pools > Pool Lists
Select the Pool in question
Click on Statistics Tab for the said Pool to view
Check Connections column and note the value for Total Connection for all Pool members
Do step 4 again after 1 hour and subtract the difference to get the delta value and compare

results.content:
# Article K000132408

1.  
2.

Description
Unable to see the Round Robin load balancing method while creating a new pool via Configuration Utility.
But when tried to create a pool from CLI it shows the Round Robin load balancing method.
Environment
BIG-IP LTM 13.X and higher
ASM provisioned
GUI
CLI
Cause
ASM evaluation add-on license got expired.
Recommended Actions
There are 2 options: 
A) Reactivate the license with a valid ASM add-on key and the issue should auto-resolve.
: Traffic processing is briefly interrupted while the BIG-IP system reloads the configuration during licenseImportant
reactivation.
 
B)  Remove Expired ASM Add-on License and Re-activate it.
Login to your Big-IP by CLI.
Run the command tmsh show /sys license detail
Locate a line similar to this: timelimited_module [Advanced Web Application Firewall,
r5XXX|XYZXYZX-ASDASDFI|20250509|20250509]
In this case, is the add-on.XYZXYZX-ASDASDF 
Go to the BIG-IP license management section (System  ››  License ).
Click on "Re-activate".
Locate the expired ASM add-on license ( ). XYZXYZX-ASDASDF
Delete the entry for the expired add-on key.
After removing the expired add-on, re-activate your base BIG-IP license.
This will update system licensing and restore access to all standard load balancing methods.
Additional Information
If you face any issues after re-provisioning the ASM license then please contact F5 global support for further support.

Retrieving list of expired SSL certificates via CLI

# run /sys crypto check-cert verbose disabled ignore-large-cert-bundles enabled

root@(johnsmith-bigip1)(cfg-sync Standalone)(/Common)(tmos)# run sys crypto check-cert
verbose disabled ignore-large-cert-bundles enabled
emailAddress=john smith@f5 com,CN=www example com,OU=Global Services,O=F5. . . .
Inc.,L=Seattle,ST=WA,C=US in file /Common/www example com_2021 expired on Dec 24 08:16:06. .
2021 GMT
root@(johnsmith-bigip1)(cfg-sync Standalone)(/Common)(tmos)#
To return a list of expired certificates in a non-Common partition, you can navigate to that partition from the TMOS
prompt using  and then run the above command.cd /<partition-name>

results.content:
# Article K16951115

Topic
You should consider using these procedures under the following conditions:
You want to renew or replace the BIG-IP system device certificate.
You want to use the Configuration utility to perform the required tasks.
If BIG-IP DNS (GTM) is provisioned, you should have less than four BIG-IP DNS systems in a DNS
synchronization (sync) group configuration.
If you have four or more BIG-IP DNS systems in a DNS sync group, using the command line is more efficient. For more
information, refer to one of the following articles:
K6353: Updating an SSL device certificate on a BIG-IP system
K9114: Creating a new SSL device certificate and key pair
Description
The BIG-IP system device certificate is an SSL certificate used to secure connections to the Configuration utility and to
secure iQuery communication between BIG-IP DNS systems. The BIG-IP system device certificate can be either
renewed or replaced with a self-signed or third-party-signed SSL certificate. The procedure you follow to update a device
certificate depends on whether the BIG-IP system is a standalone system or a peer member in a DNS sync group.
Standalone system
Standalone systems use device certificates to secure connections to the Configuration utility when managing the BIG-IP
system. If your BIG-IP system is a standalone device, you can use one of the following procedures to renew or
replace the device certificate: 
Renew the device certificate
Replace the device certificate

OAuth Client and Resource Server

  authorization servers that APM supports
Access Policy Manager (APM) supports OAuth 2.0 only. When configured as an

AzureAD - Azure Active
    Directory

support.f5.com
.
APM also supports the configuration of custom providers, that is, external
   OAuth 2.0 authorization servers that F5 has not tested. Custom providers should comply with RFC
   6749.
* Considerations for the Okta authorization server
The Okta default authorization server does not publish the access token signing key. Instead,
    you need to use a custom authorization server that will publish the access token signing key on
    the respective jwks_uri. You can identify a custom authorization server by looking at the issuer
    value.
For example, if the issuer is:
https://f5test1.oktapreview.com
then the issuer is the built-in OIDC authorization server, and the access token cannot be
    validated locally.
If the issuer is:
https://partnerpoc.oktapreview.com/oauth2

Access Policy Item Reference
About logon
  items
Logon items either display on a logon screen, or specify and present a logon
   screen to a user.
Only the
   Virtual Keyboard item displays on a logon screen.
Logon screens display input fields, and in some cases messages. The items
   that present a logon screen accept user input and store it in session variables for use in
   another access policy item; typically, that is an authentication item and it usually follows a
   logon item in an access policy.
When you work with a logon item, you can usually change some aspect of the
   logon experience.
Language on the screen
Access Policy Manager (APM) provides the text displayed on the logon screen
     translated into a number of languages. (Languages are specified in the access profile.)
     Selecting a language in a logon item translates the text to that language. Translated text can
     be used as is or customized further.
Text on the screen
APM provides default labels for the user input fields and for any
     messages that can be displayed on the logon screen. The text can be edited.
Fields on the screen
The logon page item provides up to five fields that can be displayed or
     not. The type for each field is user-selectable: password, text, select (from a list).
Some logon items include authenticatio

Known Issue
Deleting a BIG-IP APM access profile may fail.
This issue occurs when all of the following conditions are met:
Your BIG-IP APM system is configured with an access profile that references customization group files.
You attempt to delete the access profile.
One or more of the customization group files are deleted.
The imported access policy includes LDAP or AD queries.

Deleting the access profile fails.

results.title: K000149249: Renew BIGIP device certificate with third-party CA and
results.content:
# Article K000149249

1.  
Description
When renew BIGIP devices certificate which running BIG-IP DNS to existing device sync groups, the devices fail to sync
properly. The issue is related to SSL certificate verification failures. Error logs indicate SSL verification failures during the
certificate exchange process between BIG-IP DNS systems.
Environment
BIG-IP DNS
All software versions
Renew certificate with using third-party CA certificates
GSLB
Cause
The root cause of the issue is expired or stale SSL certificates in the Trusted Device Certificates and Trusted Server
Certificates directories on the BIG-IP DNS systems. Additionally, the third-party CA certificates may need to be correctly
imported into the Trusted Server Certificates section if secure connections within GSLB are being used.
Recommendations
Remove Old Certificates:
Log in to the Configuration utility of the BIG-IP DNS device where you installed the new device certificate.
Navigate to the Certificate Management Section:
In the navigation menu, go to  >  >  > System Certificate Management Traffic Certificate Management
.SSL Certificates
A list of all installed certificates will be displayed.
Identify and Delete the Old Certificate:
Locate the certificate you wish to remove.
Ensure the certificate is not currently in use by any SSL profiles or services. The BIG-IP system will
prevent deletion of certificates that are in use.
Select the certificate and click .Delete

results.title: K10683
results.content:
# Article K10683

Known Issue
This is the result of a known issue. When you generate and install a self-signed client root CA certificate on the FirePass
controller, the corresponding certificate revocation list (CRL) is generated the first time you delete a client certificate. The
expiration date of the CRL is set to one year ahead of the current date, and its expiration date is updated each time you
delete a client certificate. For example, if you delete a client certificate on 10/28/2008, FirePass sets the expiration date
of the CRL to 10/28/2009. 
If you do not delete any client certificates for a year, the CRL expires. This issue causes users with client certificates
(including users with valid client certificates) to fail the SSL handshake with the FirePass controller. When this issue
occurs, FirePass logs an error message that appears similar to the following example to the  onServer error log (https)
the Reports: HTTP Logs page:
[error] mod_ssl: Certificate Verification: Error (12): CRL has expired
F5 Networks Product Development tracked this issue as CR72872 and it was fixed in FirePass versions 6.0.2 and 6.1.0.
For information about upgrading, refer to the  Release Notes.FirePass
This issue was fixed in cumulative hotfix HF-552-11 for FirePass version 5.5.2. You may download this hotfix or later
versions of the cumulative hotfix from the F5 Networks  site.Downloads
Important: In FirePass versions 6.0.2 and later, and in FirePass version 5.5.2 with cumulative hotfix

results.title: K7820: Overview of SNAT features
results.content:
# Article K000156535

Related Content
K7820: Overview of SNAT features
K47945399: Create basic SNATs and SNAT pools
Description
When performing SSL handshake between a client and F5 BIG-IP LTM, followed by F5 to server, connections may be
reset by the BIG-IP after certificate exchange. Packet captures show successful client-side connection establishment,
but the BIG-IP resets the connection to the client after failing to initiate a server-side connection. Server-side statistics
indicate zero packets/bytes out, and TCP retransmit timeouts are observed. Example log:
[F5RST(peer): TCP retransmit timeout]
Environment
BIG-IP
SNAT pool
Cause
The SNAT pool member IP address used for source translation is not configured as a Self IP on the BIG-IP device and is
not bound to any VLAN. The BIG-IP cannot source traffic from this address, resulting in failure to establish server-side
connections and causing connection resets after SSL handshake.
Recommended Actions
Define a new Self IP address on the BIG-IP device that includes the SNAT pool member IP.
Ensure the Self IP is explicitly associated with the VLAN corresponding to the egress interface (the interface
through which BIG-IP routes traffic to pool members).
Alternatively, modify the virtual server configuration to use SNAT Automap. Automap ensures that BIG-IP selects
a valid Self IP on the egress VLAN for SNAT translation, eliminating dependency on an incorrectly defined or
un-associated SNAT pool address.
Additional Information
No

results.content:
F5 Bug ID 599567: APM assumes SNAT automap, does not use SNAT pool

Severity: 3-Major | Opened: Jun 16, 2016 | Fixed In: 15.0.0, 14.1.2.5, 14.0.1.1, 13.1.1.5, 12.1.5 | Affected Versions: 12.1.0, 12.1.0 HF1, 12.1.0 HF2, 12.1.1, 12.1.1 HF1, 12.1.1 HF2, 12.1.2, 12.1.2 HF1, 12.1.2 HF2, 12.1.3, 12.1.3.1, 12.1.3.2, 12.1.3.3, 12.1.3.4, 12.1.3.5, 12.1.3.6, 12.1.3.7, 12.1.4, 12.1.4.1, 13.0.0, 13.0.0 HF1, 13.0.0 HF2, 13.0.0 HF3, 13.0.1, 13.1.0, 13.1.0.1, 13.1.0.2, 13.1.0.3, 13.1.0.4, 13.1.0.5, 13.1.0.6, 13.1.0.7, 13.1.0.8, 13.1.1, 13.1.1.2, 13.1.1.3, 13.1.1.4, 14.0.0, 14.0.0.1, 14.0.0.2, 14.0.0.3, 14.0.0.4, 14.0.0.5, 14.0.1, 14.1.0, 14.1.0.1, 14.1.0.2, 14.1.0.3, 14.1.0.5, 14.1.0.6, 14.1.2, 14.1.2.1, 14.1.2.2, 14.1.2.3, 14.1.2.4

Symptoms: When a virtual server configured to use a SNAT pool is also associated with APM (for example, when configured as a RDP gateway), the SNAT pool setting is not honored. Also SNAT configuration of 'None' does not work. It always works as if it is configured with Automap.

Conditions: SNAT pool configured. -- APM configured (one example is deploying the Horizon View iApp for APM).

Impact: The VLAN Self IP address is used instead of the SNAT pool addresses.

Workaround: First, follow the configuration details in K03113285: Overview of BIG-IP APM layered virtual servers :: https://support.f5.com/csp/article/K03113285, to ensure everything is configured properly. Then ensure that the appropriate SNAT pool is set on the new layered forwarding virtual sever.

Symptoms: When a snat pool member is deleted, POOL_FLAG_HOMOGENEOUS is not re-set properly when the pool member is deleted.

Conditions: -- SNAT pool with two members in multiple networks -- One pool member is deleted such that all SNAT pool members are on the same network.

Description
This article provides a comprehensive guide to all available methods for establishing communication between the BIG-IP
system and pool members. It covers configuration steps, use cases, best practices, and relevant KB article references
for each method.
Environment
BIG-IP LTM (All versions)
Classic BIG-IP (versions 9.x - 17.x, 21.x)
F5OS-based systems (rSeries, VELOS)
All deployment scenarios (on-premises, cloud, hybrid)
Cause
The BIG-IP system requires network connectivity to pool members for load balancing and health monitoring. While the
most common method is configuring a self IP on the same subnet as pool members, there are multiple alternative
approaches depending on network architecture, security requirements, and deployment scenarios.
This article covers  for BIG-IP to communicate with pool members:11 different methods

Route Domains

Forwarding Virtual Servers

# Article K15586

Known Issue
SNAT Automap may select a source address that is not attached to the egress VLAN.
This issue occurs when all of the following conditions are met:
A virtual server uses SNAT Automap.
A static route exists that matches IP addresses in VLAN_A, but whose gateway resides in VLAN_B.
A more specific route exists, which matches a VLAN_A pool member, whose gateway resides in VLAN_A.
Example VLAN settings
VLAN Self IP Address Subnet Mask / Bits
VLAN_A 10.0.0.1 255.255.254.0 or /23 (hosts 10.0.0.1 - 10.0.1.254)

Destination Subnet Mask / Bits Gateway
10.0.1.0 255.255.255.0 or /24 10.0.3.254
10.0.1.10 255.255.255.255 or /32 10.0.1.254

by way of gateway  and traffic destined for host IP address  to egress the  by way of10.0.3.253 10.0.1.10 VLAN_A

SNAT source address for traffic destined to host IP address  within .10.0.1.10 VLAN_A
As a result, traffic destined for IP address  egresses   but has the source IP address of the self IP10.0.1.10 VLAN_A

results.title: K13622: BIG-IP DNS HTTP health monitor behavior when processing a server response (9.x - 15.0.x)
results.content:
# Article K56163641

Topic
This article applies to BIG-IP 15.1.x through 16.x. For information about other versions, refer to the following article:
K13622: BIG-IP DNS HTTP health monitor behavior when processing a server response (9.x - 15.0.x)
You can use the BIG-IP DNS (formerly BIG-IP GTM) HTTP and HTTPS health monitors to verify the health of the server
by checking whether the expected response is returned after a specific request. The following information provides
insight into how the BIG-IP DNS HTTP and HTTPS health monitors behave upon receiving different responses from a
server.
Description
By default, the BIG-IP DNS system uses HTTP version 0.9 when sending monitor requests, unless the HTTP
version-specific information is configured in the Send String. When the BIG-IP DNS system is configured to send HTTP
version 0.9 monitor requests, the BIG-IP DNS system marks the server up as long as the server returns a response.
When the BIG-IP DNS HTTP health monitor is configured to send either HTTP version 1.0 or 1.1 in its Send String, the
BIG-IP DNS system will first review the HTTP response status code from the server. By default the monitor expects a
HTTP   response however different and multiple response codes can be configured. If the Response code is a200 OK
match to the allowed list, it then proceeds to search for the Receive String in the server response payload (if configured).
When the Receive String is successfully found in the server response payload (or if no

results.title: K02024845: BIG-IP LTM load balancing | BIG-IP LTM-DNS operations
results.content:
# Article K02024845

Chapter 2: BIG-IP LTM load balancing
Table of contents |  | << Previous chapter Next chapter >>
BIG-IP systems are designed to distribute client connections to load balancing pools, which are typically made up of
multiple pool members. The selected load balancing method (or algorithm) determines how connections are distributed
across pool members.
Your choice of load balancing method may depend on factors such as pool member availability or session affinity:
Some load balancing methods distribute connections evenly across pool members, regardless of the current
workload of pool members or nodes. Such methods work best with homogeneous server pools and workloads.
Some load balancing methods favor higher performing servers. These methods may result in deliberately uneven
traffic distribution across pool members.
Some load balancing methods consider factors which change at run time, such as current connection count or
capacity.
Contents
Chapter sections
Load balancing types
Static load balancing methods
Dynamic load balancing methods
Priority Group Activation
Monitors
Monitor benefits

results.title: K000158134: HTTP/HTTPS Health monitor configuration and
results.content:
# Article K000158134

Description
Pool members configured with HTTP/HTTPS health monitors are unexpectedly marked down or are not being marked
down when it should be.
Some issues and/ or questions you may encounter:
Manual connectivity tests (such as curl) to the same pool members may succeed and return HTTP 200 OK.
The pool members remain available when monitored via TCP.
Packet captures show that, during failed HTTP/HTTPS health checks, the pool member completes TCP and
SSL/TLS (when the HTTPS monitor is used) handshakes.
 It may or may not respond with application data after the monitor's Send String is sent, resulting in the monitor
marking the member as unavailable.
Environment
Default and custom HTTP/HTTPS monitors in use.
Cause
There are a number of situations that can bring about a monitor response, or lack thereof, due to improper or missing
configuration of HTTP/HTTPS health monitors:
The absence of the Receive String. Without a valid Receive String, the monitor accepts any response. 
Configuration of the Send String does not elicit the proper response from the pool member
The Receive String presented by the pool member does not match what is configured in the monitor.
The pool member fails to respond to the Application Data Packet.
Using the default Http or Https monitors.
Note: There are often misconceptions about F5 monitors. A monitor is a very black and white thing. It sends out a packet
(that packet type will be based upon the type of monitor) and if it does n

results.title: K01524704: Configuring a TCP health Monitor using a binary send and
K12376: The receive string configured in an HTTP health monitor is
K350: Recommended health monitor interval and timeout values
results.content:
# Article K350

Topic
The recommended interval and timeout values for a service check by a BIG-IP health monitor vary, depending on the
type of service being checked.
An interval is measured in seconds. As a rule, the timeout should be 3 times the interval, plus one second.
For example:
Interval: 5
Timeout: 16
This timeout allows BIG-IP to send four checks and provide a reasonable amount of time to receive the reply. For
example when you have the 5 second interval with a 16 second timeout this gives the service four tries to respond: 0
sec, 5 sec, 10 sec, 15 seconds. If no response is received or the receive string doesn’t match, then at 16 seconds the
service is marked down. So if it fails to respond to the first two checks but responds at 10 seconds, then the service will
stay marked up since it responded within the timeout window.
For any given service, set the health check to a time that reflects the maximum delay in response that is acceptable for
customer access.
In an ideal environment, BIG-IP should mark a server as down if it fails to respond to a single service check, but in a
normal networking environment, it is expected that traffic spikes will occasionally cause servers to fail a service check.
The most important function of a health check is to determine if a server is completely down, or if the server has been
down for an unacceptable amount of time.
A 3:1 service check ratio is an effective setting because it provides a good balance between an acceptable amoun

# Article K13343

Topic

1. Description
BIG-IP DNS configuration changes made on one device in a sync group do not replicate to peer devices. When this
occurs, DNS objects such as wide IPs, pools, virtual servers, and other GSLB configurations fail to synchronize across
the GTM infrastructure, resulting in inconsistent configurations between sync group members.

BIG-IP DNS (GTM) version 11.x and later
Multi-device GTM sync group configuration
DNS synchronization setting disabled under DNS > Settings > GSLB > General
iQuery connections established between peer devices

The  checkbox is disabled (unchecked) under  on one or more BIG-IPSynchronize DNS > Settings > GSLB > General
DNS devices in the sync group. When this setting is disabled, the device does not participate in configuration
synchronization, preventing replication of DNS configuration changes between peer devices.

synchronization will not occur unless the  setting is ex

results.title: Resource Provisioning
K15270: Configuring a BIG-IP redundant pair in which one of the units
results.content:
# Article K15270

Topic
You should consider using this procedure under the following condition:
You want to configure a BIG-IP redundant pair in which only one of the units is licensed and provisioned for the
BIG-IP DNS module.
Description
Beginning in BIG-IP 11.x, you can configure a redundant pair configuration in which only one of the units is licensed and
provisioned for BIG-IP DNS (in previous versions you must provision BIG-IP DNS on both redundant pair members).
This allows you to deploy a mixed redundant pair configuration in which the BIG-IP DNS system responds to DNS
queries whether the system is in active or standby mode. Before deploying a mixed redundant pair configuration for a
BIG-IP DNS system, you should consider the following information:
It is only necessary to license/provision the BIG-IP DNS module on one of the units
ConfigSync operations do not synchronize the provisioning configuration to the redundant pair member
A mixed pair configuration, in which BIG-IP DNS is licensed on only one system, does not provide redundancy in
the event that the BIG-IP DNS system fails
Prerequisites
You must meet the following prerequisites to use this procedure:
You have defined the device clustering, failover, and configuration synchronization (ConfigSync) components for
the systems.
You can access the Configuration utility.
Procedures
K15270: Configuring a BIG-IP redundant pair in which one of the units
is licensed and provisioned for BIG-IP DNS
https://my.f5.com/manage

# Article K12322

Topic
 The information in this article replaces the information that was previously found in SOL10156.Note:
: In BIG-IP 11.x, resource provisioning is no longer synchronized between redundant pair members duringNote
ConfigSync operations. For more information, refer to K13533: The BIG-IP GTM module can be licensed and
provisioned on one of the devices in a redundant pair. 
If you have a BIG-IP redundant pair configuration in which both units are licensed and provisioned for BIG-IP LTM and
only one of the units is licensed and provisioned for BIG-IP GTM, you must make sure to provision the BIG-IP GTM
module on both units. If you do not provision BIG-IP GTM on both units, you may experience issues in which the
resource provisioning configuration for one unit is synchronized to the other unit.
For example, if you have a BIG-IP redundant pair in which BIGIP-1 (the active unit) is licensed and provisioned for
BIG-IP LTM and BIG-IP GTM, and BIGIP-2 (the standby unit) is licensed and provisioned for BIG-IP LTM only, and you
run ConfigSync from BIGIP-2 to BIGIP-1, BIGIP-1 will inherit BIGIP-2's BIG-IP GTM provisioning level of None
(Disabled). When the BIGIP-1 unit loses its BIG-IP GTM provisioning configuration, the module is non-functional on the
unit.
Important: Licensing the GTM module on only one unit of a BIG-IP redundant pair is not a recommended
configuration. On asymmetrically licensed systems, the non-floating self-IP of the unit on which GTM is licensed i

results.title: K02038650: The BIG-IP configuration fails to load after upgrading
results.content:
# Article K02038650

Known Issue
The BIG-IP configuration fails to load after upgrading.
This issue occurs when all of the following conditions are met:
Your BIG-IP system is licensed with BIG-IP DNS (formerly known as BIG-IP GTM).
You provision BIG-IP DNS and subsequently deprovision BIG-IP DNS.
After you deprovision BIG-IP DNS, you upgrade to later BIG-IP versions.
When you provision the BIG-IP DNS module, the BIG-IP system creates the configuration file. Whenbigip_gtm.conf 
you later deprovision BIG-IP DNS and upgrade to later versions, the remaining configuration file schema is not
recognized by the later versions.
For example, your BIG-IP system runs 12.1.3.7 and the BIG-IP DNS module is provisioned. Next, you deprovision the
BIG-IP DNS module and upgrade to 13.1.1.3. You then upgrade to 14.0.0.4, and the configuration fails to load because
the 12.1.3.7 config schema is not recognized in 14.0.0.4.
Impact
The BIG-IP configuration fails to load.
Symptoms
As a result of this issue, you may encounter one or more of the following symptoms:
The BIG-IP Configuration fails to load after upgrade with an error message that appears similar to the following
example:
01420006:3: Can't find specified cli schema data for x.x.x.x
: x.x.x.x denotes an earlier BIG-IP software version.Note
Resolution
Status

results.content:
# Article K14721

Topic
The DNS-Lite module is added on BIG-IP LTM-licensed BIG-IP platforms.
Description
DNS-Lite module is available on BIG-IP 11.4.0 through 12.1.0 as a heavily rate-limited, non-licensed, and provisioned
evaluation and testing of DNS and global server load balancing (GSLB) services at no charge.
The DNS-Lite module is added when all of the following conditions are met:
You license a BIG-IP LTM system after August 2013
You activate the BIG-IP LTM license on one of the following BIG-IP platforms:
VIPRION
Virtual Edition
10000 series
7000 series
5000 series
4000 series
2000 series
After you activate the license, the  file will include a line that appears similar to the following example:bigip.license
active module: GTM-DNS, RL, LO
Recommendations
For evaluation purposes, the DNS-Lite module provides non-production versions of the following services:
GSLB
DNS Express
Domain Name System Security Extensions (DNSSEC)
If the BIG-IP system is running 11.4.0 or later, you can provision the DNS-Lite features for evaluation purposes.
If the BIG-IP system is running 11.3.0 or earlier, the new module is added to the license file, but no new functionality is
enabled.

results.title: K9837: An incomplete iQuery mesh across a sync group may result in inconsistent responses or
results.content:
# Article K15408

Issue
Prerequisites
Before attempting to troubleshoot a BIG-IP DNS monitor (formerly known as BIG-IP GTM), if you are running multiple
BIG-IP devices, you must ensure that the iQuery mesh is complete between all devices. For more information, refer to
the following articles:
K9837: An incomplete iQuery mesh across a sync group may result in inconsistent responses or
configuration loss
K13690: Troubleshooting BIG-IP DNS synchronization and iQuery connections (11.x - 14.x)
Overview
The BIG-IP DNS health monitors verify connectivity to virtual server objects that reside on BIG-IP devices and
non-BIG-IP devices. When a BIG-IP DNS monitor marks a virtual server down, and that virtual server is a member of a
wide BIG-IP pool, the BIG-IP DNS no longer includes that virtual server's address in answers to DNS queries for a wide
IP.
The BIG-IP DNS system receives the status of virtual servers that reside on non-BIG-IP devices directly from BIG-IP
devices using the iQuery protocol. For example, if you have BIG-IP DNS devices and BIG-IP LTM devices in a data
center, along with generic host servers, the BIG-IP DNS system will dynamically assign a BIG-IP device to probe the
generic host server for status. You can also assign a specific BIG-IP device or group of devices to monitor a server
object by using prober pools. For more information about prober pools, refer to the Configuring Device-Specific
 section of the manual.Probing and Statistics Collection  BIG-IP DNS Imple

processes.
One  process is made responsible for each monitor instance, using a simple hash mechanism. Thisgtmd
distributes the monitor instances evenly across all the  processes in the sync group. The managing  isgtmd gtmd
responsible for identifying a  agent to execute the probe, and for scheduling the firing of the probesbig3d

Issue

You should consider using this procedure under the following condition:
You are experiencing BIG-IP GTM synchronization and iQuery connection issues.
Symptoms
As a result of synchronization and iQuery connection issues, you may encounter the following symptoms:
BIG-IP GTM synchronization group members may have configuration discrepancies.
Server objects are marked as Unavailable (Red) or Unknown (Blue).
Log messages report issues related to synchronization and iQuery connections.
Recommended Actions
A BIG-IP GTM synchronization group is a collection of multiple BIG-IP GTM systems that share and synchronize
configuration settings. You must meet several minimum requirements for BIG-IP GTM synchronization group members
to communicate and synchronize properly. For more information, refer to K13734: BIG-IP GTM synchronization group
.requirements
The  process on each BIG-IP GTM system will attempt to establish an iQuery connection over port 4353 with eachgtmd
self IP address defined on each server in the BIG-IP GTM configuration of type BIG-IP. If Link Discovery is enabled and
a default gateway pool is assigned as the default route on that server, the BIG-IP GTM system will also attempt to
connect to the self IP addresses assigned to the Virtual Local Area Networ

results.title: K9824
results.content:
# Article K9824

Topic
It is possible for a BIG-IP GTM to assign a BIG-IP LTM system with limited network connectivity as a prober. Limited
connectivity in this case refers to the BIG-IP LTM system's inability to probe the systems it is designated to probe, or to
send iQuery communications to remote BIG-IP GTM systems.
Although a number of conditions in a network environment may cause this type of limited connectivity, the most
commonly observed root cause is a misconfigured or missing BIG-IP LTM default gateway, or in the case of limited
iQuery communications, an SSL certificate exchange issue may be preventing establishment of a secure connection.
: The configured default gateway may not be loaded if the BIG-IP license is expired.Note
A BIG-IP GTM system that load-balances a BIG-IP LTM system with a missing or misconfigured default gateway may
result in the following scenario:
iQuery communications between the BIG-IP LTM and GTM systems on the same subnet succeed
Probe requests to systems on the same subnet as the BIG-IP LTM system succeed
iQuery communications between the BIG-IP LTM and GTM systems on another subnet (or in another data
center) may fail, resulting in inconsistent status across the BIG-IP GTM sync group for objects configured on or
probed by the affected BIG-IP LTM
Probe requests to systems beyond the local subnet may fail, resulting in a false  status across theunavailable
sync group for all affected objects
An SSL certificate exchange issue between a BIG-I

results.title: K45907236: Overview of BIG-IP DNS synchronization
results.content:
# Article K45907236

Topic
A BIG-IP DNS synchronization group (sync group) is a collection of BIG-IP DNS systems that share configuration
settings and metrics information. Changes you make to any system in the sync group are automatically propagated to all
other BIG-IP DNS systems in the group.
Description
BIG-IP DNS systems that are part of a sync group share and synchronize configuration settings and metrics information.
You should create a synchronization group if you want BIG-IP DNS systems to work together to monitor the availability
and performance of global resources and use that information to manage network traffic patterns. There is no
pre-defined limit on the maximum number of BIG-IP DNS systems allowed in a sync group.
Sync group communication
BIG-IP DNS systems in a synchronization group establish an iQuery connection to each sync group member using the
defined self IP address and TCP port 4353. When you make a change to the BIG-IP DNS configuration, the local mcpd
process forwards the new configuration to the local  process. The  process updates the configurationgtmd gtmd
timestamp and instructs the local  process to begin advertising the updated configuration to all remote big3d gtmd
processes.
For example, the following figure shows that, after a configuration change is made on the Los Angeles BIG-IP DNS
system, the local  process initiates an iQuery connection to BIG-IP DNS sync group members in New York andbig3d
Europe and advertises the updated configuratio

the sync group when installing a UCS on a device.
Description
Before installing a UCS archive on a BIG-IP DNS system that will be added to an existing sync group, note the following
information:
When the BIG-IP DNS system loads the UCS, the MCP daemon generates a new configuration change identifier
(commit ID).
The new commit ID causes the BIG-IP DNS system to synchronize the contents of the UCS archive to the BIG-IP
DNS sync group.
You may like this behavior if you want to install the UCS on a current sync group member and roll the entire BIG-IP DNS
sync group back to the configuration contained in the UCS archive.
However, in some cases you may want to install a UCS and prevent the BIG-IP DNS system from synchronizing the
contents of the UCS archive to the BIG-IP DNS sync group. For example, if you are installing a UCS on a new device,
such as an RMA replacement, you can prevent the system from synchronizing the configuration in the UCS to the sync

receiving updates from the peers system it goes into "wait for sync" state (NOT ready) and will not be resolving
the incoming queries for the duration on update period and this is the default behavior.
This can be problematic if all devices in the GTM/DNS sync group are unable to communicate via iQuery with
each other.

results.content:
# Article K75177455

1.  
2.

Description
Topology load balancing distributes DNS name resolution requests based on the proximity of the LDNS to the data
center housing the resource that responds to the request. When Topology load balancing is enabled, the BIG-IP system
uses topology records to make load balancing decisions. The following article provides and overview on how to force the
traffic from a specific region to another data center.
Environment
BIG-IP DNS
You have already configured the Topology load balancing method for a wide IP
You wish to redirect DNS traffic from a specific region to another data center
Cause
None
Recommended Actions
The Topology mode bases the distribution of requests on the topology records and the weighted scores configured for
each record. The topology records direct DNS queries to the most appropriate resources, based on geographical
information.
In the event that you need to force traffic to another location, eg, for troubleshooting purposes, you can temporarily
create a topology record with a higher weighting than existing records, as the matching topology record with the highest
weighting will be selected. To do so, perform the following procedure:
Log in to the Configuration utility.
Navigate to   >   >   >  .DNS GSLB Topology Records
Click Create
On the Topology Record Builder page, select  >   from the Region is  > Region_to_modify Request Source
 menus.

results.title: K13412: Overview of BIG-IP DNS Topology records (11.x - 14.x)
results.content:
# Article K14421

Topic
This article describes split DNS, deployment scenarios, and how to obtain split DNS behavior with the BIG-IP DNS
system through wide IPs and topology load balancing.
Description
Split DNS refers to setting up different DNS views for the same domain. This configuration involves creating one zone to
resolve names for the internal network and another zone to resolve names for the external network.
: For more information about resolving names for internal networks, refer to the Note Internet Engineering Task Force
. This link takes you to a resource outside of AskF5, and it is possible that the document may be removed(RFC 1918)
without our knowledge.
Split DNS implementation using standard DNS servers
Split DNS is a configuration in which the resolution IP address of a name is dependent on the location of the requesting
client, or the Local Domain Name System (LDNS) is being requested on behalf of a client. For example, a site may have
several web applications that have both internal network addresses and external addresses. Clients on the internal
network may have access to both sets of applications. The external applications are accessible using a Network Address
Translation (NAT). The intent of split DNS is to have internal clients resolve fully-qualified domain names (FQDN) for the
applications to internal addresses on the corporate LAN, rather than accessing the resource through the advertised
public address for that resource. Split DNS also ensures t

results.content:
# Article K13412

Topic
This article applies to BIG-IP DNS 11.x through 17.x. For information about other versions, refer to the following article:
K10434: Overview of BIG-IP GTM Topology records (9.x - 10.x)
Summary
Topology is a proximity based load balancing mode that allows you to direct traffic by defining topology records and
selecting the Topology load-balancing mode for the wide IP or pool. The Topology mode bases the distribution of
requests on the topology records and the weighted scores configured for each record. The topology records direct DNS
queries to the closest virtual server, based on geographical information. The BIG-IP DNS system is designed to support
an unlimited number of topology records. The only limit is the amount of memory available to the  process.gtmd
Description
To understand topology records, read the following sections:
Topology record format
Topology records matching modes and the importance of order
Topology record creation
Topology record format
Topology records are stored in the  file on the BIG-IP DNS system and are used to match a local domainbigip_gtm.conf
name system (LDNS) request with a specific destination object. A topology record has the following fields: an LDNS
request source statement, a destination statement, an operator, and a weight.
LDNS request source
The LDNS request source statement defines the origin of a name resolution request. For example, the topology
statement can be defined to direct an LDNS request from a partic

results.title: K6555
results.content:
# Article K6555

Known Issue
This is the result of a known issue. All FirePass Network Access clients require a DNS name resolution of the FirePass
hostname or fully qualified domain name (FQDN) used to log in to the FirePass controller.
In addition to the initial DNS name resolution, one or more DNS name resolutions are required when Network Access is
activated. If the IP address returned by the first name resolution belongs to a different FirePass controller than the IP
addresses returned in subsequent resolutions, the Network Access connection fails, and produces an Invalid Session
 error.ID
This error indicates the Network Access client is attempting to connect to a different FirePass than the one which
serviced the user's logon. The Network Access client presents session ID credentials that were generated by, and only
valid for, the first FirePass controller, to the second FirePass controller. The second FirePass controller rejects the
credentials and the connection fails.
When this failure occurs, the BIG-IP GTM has given the Network Access client different answers to its DNS name
resolution queries for the FirePass FQDN. By default, BIG-IP GTM performs load balancing by returning different
answers (IP addresses) to name resolution queries for the FirePass FQDN (the wide IP in the BIG-IP GTM
configuration). Additional configuration for the BIG-IP GTM load balancing algorithm is necessary to ensure that only one
IP address is given to the Network Access client in answer

A BIG-IP GTM configuration is comprised of the different components that make up the physical and logical segments of
the network. These components are stored in the BIG-IP GTM configuration files and represent the configuration that the
BIG-IP GTM uses to direct DNS traffic to the best available resource.

the BIG-IP GTM configuration files directly, you should check the syntax after saving the changes by running the

The wideip.conf file
The  file is the main BIG-IP GTM configuration file. It contains the components that comprise the physicalwideip.conf
network, such as data centers, servers, and links, and the components that comprise the logical network, such as wide
IPs, pools, and global settings. Most of the configuration components you build using the Configuration utility are saved
to the  file.wideip.conf

results.title: K000135464: Considerations when using FQDN pool members
results.content:
# Article K000135464

Description
A pool may be created that includes static IP pool members and (fully-qualified domain name) FQDN nodes.
Upon creating a new FQDN node, the BIG-IP system will automatically query its configured DNS resolver for both its A
and AAAA records. It will collect IPv4 and IPv6 addresses that are recursively queried by the DNS resolver.
For example, the following shows a query for www f5 com retuns 2 IPv4 addresses and 2 IPv6 addresses.. .
# dig www f5 com A. .
www f5 com.       8    IN   CNAME  dwbfwz8xncgmg.cloudfront.net.. .
dwbfwz8xncgmg cloudfront net. 38 IN   A    108.156.91.78. .
dwbfwz8xncgmg cloudfront net. 38 IN   A    108.156.91.96. .
dwbfwz8xncgmg cloudfront net. 48 IN   AAAA  2600:9000:2351:9200:14:232e:8a00:93a1. .
dwbfwz8xncgmg cloudfront net. 48 IN   AAAA  2600:9000:2351:7c00:14:232e:8a00:93a1. .
The BIG-IP system will then proceed to automatically create nodes and pool members consisting of all the returned DNS
A and AAAA IP address records. These are dynamically created and removed based on the DNS serevr response.
These automatically created nodes and pool members are also referred to as  nodes or  poolephemeral ephemeral
members.
For example, the following pool has two static pool members and one FQDN node.
# tmsh list ltm pool my_http_pool
ltm pool my_http_pool {
  members {
    10.11.65.100:http {
      address 10.11.65.100
      ratio 5
      session monitor-enabled
      state down
    }
    10.11.65.101:http {
      address 10

results.title: K14510: Overview of DNS query processing on BIG-IP systems
results.content:
# Article K14510

Topic
When you license and configure the BIG-IP system to use various Domain Name System (DNS) features, it is beneficial
to understand the order in which the system processes DNS queries.
Description
The BIG-IP system offers the following DNS-related features to answer DNS queries:
DNS iRules
DNS iRules include events and commands that specifically work with DNS queries on BIG-IP DNS (12.x and
later), BIG-IP GTM (11.x), and BIG-IP LTM (11.x and later) systems. DNS iRule events, such as DNS_REQUEST
and DNS_RESPONSE, can modify DNS packet attributes, answer directly, or load balance requests. DNS iRule
events and commands require a GTM or DNS Services license.
Wide IPs
A BIG-IP DNS (12.x and later) wide IP responds to DNS A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, NAPTR, and SRV
record queries using load-balancing algorithms, topology-based routing, and iRules to distribute traffic in a
manner that best suits the network or application. BIG-IP DNS wide IP functionality requires a BIG-IP
DNS license.

results.title: as3-schema
BIG-IP Inter-Module HTTP and APM Event Flow (F5 DevCentral)
results.content:
BIG-IP Inter-Module HTTP and APM Event Flow (based on F5 DevCentral diagram, 2016)

This document describes the order in which BIG-IP modules and iRule events fire for an HTTP request, including the Access Policy Manager (APM) policy evaluation flow.

== CLIENT-SIDE REQUEST FLOW ==

1. CLIENT_ACCEPTED
   Fires when a new TCP connection is accepted from the client. This is the first event in the
   clientside context. TCP payload inspection begins here.

2. Valid HTTP request check
   BIG-IP inspects the TCP payload. If the request is not valid HTTP, processing stops.

3. HTTP_REQUEST
   Fires for each valid HTTP request. This is the primary iRule event for clientside request
   processing. After HTTP_REQUEST:
   - If HTTP::collect is called explicitly, HTTP_REQUEST_DATA fires once the specified number
     of bytes (or the full body) is collected.
   - If no collect is pending, processing continues immediately.

4. Cache module check
   BIG-IP checks for a cache hit. If there is a cache match, CACHE_REQUEST fires.
   On a cache hit, CACHE_RESPONSE fires and the cached response is returned to the client,
   bypassing the server-side flow.

5. ASM (Advanced WAF) module check
   If an ASM policy is attached, ASM inspects the request:
   - If an attack is detected or a policy violation occurs, ASM_REQUEST_VIOLATION fires.
   - If L7 DoS is detected, IN_DOSL7_ATTACK fires and DoS mitigation is applied.
   - If the request is blocked, ASM_REQUEST_BLOCKING fires and a module-specifi

results.title: HTTP Event Order -- Access Policy Manager
results.content:
HTTP Event Order -- Access Policy Manager
Early last year I blogged an update to the iRules HTTP event order that F5er John Alam put together. John is back with another update to the drawing, this time, however, including the access policy manager flow. This article will cover the APM events as well as a couple new standard events added back in 11.0.
APM Event Flow
There are a plethora of events and commands specific to the access policy manager in iRules,
which you can find here on Clouddocs
. I’ll focus on the events in this article. Of the eight events, there are only a few events triggered per request.
ACCESS_ACL_ALLOWED – triggered right after HTTP_REQUEST (and before CACHE_REQUEST, not included in this drawing) for a request that has been allowed by ACLs. ACCESS checks the request for a valid session, valid policy result, and then evaluates ACLs. If ACL allows a request, it raises ACCESS_ACL_ALLOWED before releasing the request to the upper layers.
ACCESS_ACL_DENIED – same process as ACCESS_ACL_ALLOWED, but triggered before the request is denied (by redirecting the request to access denied page)
REWRITE_REQUEST_DONE – triggered right after ACCESS_ACL_ALLOWED when a portal access resource is accessed.
The other events are only triggered at some specific processing for a user’s session and access policy. For the ACCESS_POLICY_* events, even though the user’s flow is utilized, the events are triggered in the context of the APM internal request as dictated by the policy.
AC

results.title: K10354610: Firewall rules | BIG-IP AFM operations guide
results.content:
# Article K10354610

Chapter 3: Firewall rules
Table of contents |  | << Previous chapter Next chapter >>
The BIG-IP AFM Network Firewall uses rules to specify traffic handling actions. Rules are collected in policies, which the
system applies at the global context, to a route domain, to a virtual server, or to a self IP address. Rules for the
management port do not require a policy but are defined directly in the management port context.
The BIG-IP system itself provides some access control measures: it drops or rejects packets that do not match a
listener. Additionally, application profiles, such as SIP, add more limits regarding the type of permitted traffic.
The BIG-IP AFM system provides the following control features:
Network Firewall provides full-featured access control lists (ACLs).
IP Intelligence provides host-based controls paired with automation.
Protocol Security provides fine-grained controls for the DNS and HTTP protocols.
Contents
Chapter sections
Network Firewall
Network firewall components
Dual stack support
IP Intelligence

Dynamic whitelist/blacklist

results.content:
# Article K61529390

1.  
2.  
3.  
4.

6.  
7.  
8.  
9.

13.  
14.  
Description
Generic Routing Encapsulation (GRE) is a tunneling protocol that can encapsulate a wide variety of network layer
protocols inside virtual point-to-point links over an IP network. Because GRE allows huge payloads to be encapsulated
and sent, adding the processing overhead of IP defragmentation to impact a target, it is abused and used in GRE Flood
attacks.  
BIG-IP AFM rules can recognize GRE traffic and limit it to intended end points, and they can be configured to allow GRE
traffic to intended end points or drop unwanted GRE Flood traffic. 
Environment
BIG-IP AFM module is licensed and provisioned
Cause
You suspect your BIG-IP system is receiving a GRE Flood attack.
Recommended Actions
You can configure an AFM rule in an existing AFM network firewall policy to drop GRE Flood traffic. To do so, perform
the following procedure:
Log in to the Configuration utility.
Navigate to  >   >  .Security Network Firewall Active Rules
Click .Add Rule
For , enter the name of the AFM Rule.Name
For , specify a position of the rule, Last, Before.. or After.. (an existing in rule in the policy), or First.Order
For , under , select .Protocol Other Protocols GRE
For , specify a Source IP Address - a single IP, Address Range or List  or Country/Region, then click Source Add
. A VLAN or tunnel can also be specified. 
For , specify a Destination IP Address or Country/Region.    Destination

results.title: K14459: The BIG-IP system sends high-speed logs through TMM interfaces only
results.content:
# Article K15368

Topic
The BIG-IP AFM system logs network firewall events using the logging profile associated with the network firewall rule.
Description
You can configure the BIG-IP AFM system to log network firewall events using the following two components:
The  setting for a network firewall rule.Logging
When you configure a network firewall rule, you can either enable or disable logging by configuring its Logging
setting. When enabled, the BIG-IP AFM system sends the details of an event that matches this rule to the
associated logging profile for further processing.
The logging profile.
The logging profile controls how the system handles logging. You can configure the logging profile to send logs
either locally or remotely. You can configure the logging profile to log only certain types of rule matches, for
example, to log only traffic that the system rejects by using a rule. You can also configure the logging profile to
log certain types of errors, for example, to log traffic containing TCP errors.
Understanding the association between the logging profile and the network firewall rule context
A network firewall rule is applied to objects in the following context:
Global
Route domain
Virtual server
Self IP
Management port
By default, all network firewall rules that the system applies to objects in the global, route domain, self IP, and
management port contexts are associated with the default  logging profile. However, by default, the global-network
 logging profile is

results.title: K000151189: Viewing the AFM Rule for Zero Hit
results.content:
# Article K000151189

1.  
Description
AFM zero hit or stale rule refers to a situation in F5 BIG-IP Advanced Firewall Manager (AFM) where a firewall
rule or policy shows a hit count of zero. This means that, according to the system statistics, no traffic has
matched (or "hit") that rule since it was deployed or since the counters were last reset.
 
Common causes for zero hit count:
The rule is not correctly positioned in the rule list, so other rules are matching the traffic first.
The rule's criteria (source, destination, protocol, etc.) do not match any current traffic.
There may be a configuration or routing issue preventing traffic from reaching the rule.
In rare cases, software bugs can cause the hit count to remain at zero even when traffic should match
the rule, e.g., .K73247815
 
CAUTION: If you intend to remove a rule that is infrequently or never hit, ensure that doing so will not create a
security issue, and that a backup has been created beforehand. A rule may be triggered rarely, but it could still
be a critical part of your security posture against specific or uncommon attacks.
 
Environment
BIG-IP AFM
 
Cause
None
 
Recommended Actions
To view the zero hit (stale) rules: 
 
Via GUI/Configuration Utility:
K000151189: Viewing the AFM Rule for Zero Hit

results.title: K000092915: BIG-IP fails to communicate with NTP servers via
results.content:
# Article K000092915

Description
Beginning in BIG-IP 14.1.0 you can configure network firewall rules to limit access to the management interface on the
BIG-IP system. The management interface network firewall rules use the same syntax as network firewall rules in BIG-IP
AFM but do not require the BIG-IP AFM module to be licensed and provisioned.
For example, you can configure a positive security posture by creating rules that allow access to specific ports on the
management interface from specific hosts on your network. The last rule you create in the rule list is an inclusive rule
that drops all traffic destined to the management interface. As a result, the BIG-IP system drops any traffic that does not
match an allow rule.
However, if you have created a deny-all rule, but no explicit allow rule for NTP, you may find the symptoms below:
The output from the ntpq command may appear similar to the following example, which indicates the
local ntpd process fails to communicate with an NTP peer server. 
[device1@bigip1:Peer Time Out of Sync:Changes Pending] ~ # ntpq -np
remote           refid      st t when poll reach   delay   offset  jitter
==============================================================================
192.168.10.67         .INIT.     16 u    - 1024    0    0.000    0.000   0.000
192.168.19.23         .INIT.     16 u    - 1024    0    0.000    0.000   0.000
In addition, you may find more explanation of each field in the output in K10240: Verifying NTP peer server

results.content:
Manual Chapter
:
Detecting and Mitigating DoS/DDoS Attacks on Protected Objects
Applies To:
Show
Versions
BIG-IP AFM
17.1.0
Detecting and Mitigating DoS/DDoS Attacks on Protected Objects
Overview: Mitigating DoS/DDoS Attacks on Protected Objects
When you create virtual servers using BIG-IP Local Traffic Manager (LTM), AFM discovers them as protected objects. This  discovery makes  it easy for you to locate and apply DoS/DDoS protection profiles,  and to view the current attack status of each protected object.
DoS/DDoS Protection Profiles
DoS/DDoS protection profiles define the strategies used to detect, and mitigate DoS/DDoS attacks on  protected objects. Protection profiles allow you to enable, and configure a wide variety of attack signatures for Network, DNS, and SIP protocols. For most attack signatures, you can allow AFM to manage detection and mitigation thresholds, or you  configure attack signature settings manually.
Protection Settings
You can apply the following protection settings to  protected objects:
Throughput Capacity
A maximum allowable throughput, in megabits per second, for the protected object. Infinite means no limit.
Protection Profile
A DoS protection profile configured to detect and mitigate DoS/DDoS attack based on known and discovered attack signatures.
Eviction Policy
An Eviction policy controlling the amount of allowable connections, based on specified high and low water marks. Once the high water mark is triggered, a number of eviction strategies

results.title: K49869231: Denial of Service | BIG-IP AFM operations guide
results.content:
# Article K49869231

Chapter 5: Denial of Service
Table of contents |  | << Previous chapter Next chapter >>
The BIG-IP AFM system provides mitigation techniques against DoS/DDoS attacks.
Denial of Service (DoS) attacks are attempts to render a machine or network resource unavailable to its intended users.
Most network DoS attacks occur at OSI layers 3, 4, or 7.
The attacks work by overwhelming the server resources by directing traffic at a particular IP address and/or port with an
inordinate amount of either legal traffic or malformed requests that exhaust available resources. For example, targeting
of the number of concurrent L4 connections or available bandwidth are just a couple of common DoS attacks. The result
is service denial to legitimate users.

Table of contents |  | << Previous chapter Next chapter >>
The BIG-IP AFM Network Firewall uses rules to specify traffic handling actions. Rules are collected in policies, which the
system applies at the global context, to a route domain, to a virtual server, or to a self IP address. Rules for the
management port do not require a policy but are defined directly in the management port context.
The BIG-IP system itself provides some access control measures: it drops or rejects packets that do not match a
listener. Additionally, application profiles, such as SIP, add more limits regarding the type of permitted traffic.
The BIG-IP AFM system provides the following control features:
Network Firewall provides full-featured access control lists (ACLs).
IP Intelligence provides host-based controls paired with automation.
Protocol Security provides fine-grained controls for the DNS and HTTP protocols.

results.title: BIG-IP Inter-Module HTTP and APM Event Flow (F5 DevCentral)
results.content:
BIG-IP Inter-Module HTTP and APM Event Flow (based on F5 DevCentral diagram, 2016)

This document describes the order in which BIG-IP modules and iRule events fire for an HTTP request, including the Access Policy Manager (APM) policy evaluation flow.

== CLIENT-SIDE REQUEST FLOW ==

1. CLIENT_ACCEPTED
   Fires when a new TCP connection is accepted from the client. This is the first event in the
   clientside context. TCP payload inspection begins here.

2. Valid HTTP request check
   BIG-IP inspects the TCP payload. If the request is not valid HTTP, processing stops.

3. HTTP_REQUEST
   Fires for each valid HTTP request. This is the primary iRule event for clientside request
   processing. After HTTP_REQUEST:
   - If HTTP::collect is called explicitly, HTTP_REQUEST_DATA fires once the specified number
     of bytes (or the full body) is collected.
   - If no collect is pending, processing continues immediately.

4. Cache module check
   BIG-IP checks for a cache hit. If there is a cache match, CACHE_REQUEST fires.
   On a cache hit, CACHE_RESPONSE fires and the cached response is returned to the client,
   bypassing the server-side flow.

5. ASM (Advanced WAF) module check
   If an ASM policy is attached, ASM inspects the request:
   - If an attack is detected or a policy violation occurs, ASM_REQUEST_VIOLATION fires.
   - If L7 DoS is detected, IN_DOSL7_ATTACK fires and DoS mitigation is applied.
   - If the request is blocked, ASM_REQUEST_BLOCKING fires and a module-specifi

results.title: K09115252: High availability | BIG-IP APM operations guide
results.content:
# Article K09115252

Chapter 7: High availability
Table of contents |  | << Previous chapter Next chapter >>
A high availability (HA) deployment consists of two BIG-IP systems synchronized with the same configuration: one
system actively processes traffic while the other remains in standby mode until needed. The goal of such redundant
pairing is to provide users with seamless, uninterrupted service in the event of failure on one device.
If the active system is taken offline or something occurs to prevent it from processing traffic, the standby system
immediately takes over. Typically, the newly active system remains active until an event requires the first BIG-IP system
to become active again or until you manually force that system into standby.
While BIG-IP system configurations allow for configurations with multiple standby systems or active-active pairings,
BIG-IP APM only supports two systems paired in active-standby configuration.
For more information, refer to  .K15503: BIG-IP APM HA considerations

Overview: Setting up F5
    Guided Configuration for SSL Orchestrator in high availability
This section describes how to deploy F5 SSL Orchestrator high availability
      (HA). SSL Orchestrator HA configuration and deployment ensures a decrease in downtime and
      eliminates single points of failure. The deployment of SSL Orchestratorâs HA works with the
      BIG-IP device groups support to sync the SSL Orchestrator specific configuration items and is
      transparent to the user.
The deployment occurs after completing a configuration change and
      selecting Deploy. The deployment request is first routed to one of the devices in the HA
      device group. This first device configures the device where the request is received. After
      successful deployment on that device, the request is repeated on other BIG-IP devices.
With SSL Orchestrator installed onto a dedicated system with failover, it
      automatically takes over in case of system failure. Data is synchronized between the two
      systems, ensuring high availability and consistent protection.
SSL Orchestrator high
        availability deployment is supported for use only with SSL Orchestrator versions 2.1 and
        later.

results.title: K4918: Overview of the F5 critical issue hotfix policy
results.content:
# Article K32528500

Known Issue
Manually synced Sync-Failover device groups fail to sync in the remote device.
This issue occurs when all of the following conditions are met:
You configure multiple devices within a traffic group to use high availability (HA) order as a fail over mechanism.
You also configure the Sync-Failover device groups for manual synchronization.
You attempt to remove a device that resides within the HA order traffic group from another device within the
Sync-Failover device group.
For example:
You configure the TG1 traffic group to use HA_ORDER as a Sync-Failover mechanism. The HA_ORDER list for TG1
includes DEV1, DEV2, and DEV3.
You want to remove DEV3 from Sync-Failover device group on DEV1. However, validation running on DEV1 prevents
you from doing so until you remove DEV3 from the TG1 HA_ORDER list.
The conflict occurs at this point. You are removing a device from a Sync-Failover group that is part of the device trust
group, which always syncs automatically. However, when manual sync is configured for the Sync-Failover device group,
the operation to remove DEV3 from the TG1 HA_ORDER list is synced as part of a manual sync operation.
After you remove DEV3 from the TG1 HA_ORDER list, the configurations don't match: The TG1 HA_ORDER list on
DEV1 does not contain DEV3, but the lists in the other trust group members do. If you don't manually sync now, before
you try to remove DEV3 from the DEV1 Sync-Failover device group, the remote trust group syncs fai

results.title: K000158917: Four Active/Active load balancing examples with F5
results.content:
# Article K000158917

Description
The question often exist about how to achieve High Availability (HA) and Synchronization (sync) with a BIG-IP in Azure.
Environment
This is a condensed version of Four Active/Active load balancing examples with F5 BIG-IP and Azure Load
 written by Michael O'Leary on the subject of synchronization of configuration objects in Azure between BIG-IPBalancer
Load Balancers. With links to other articles (Related Articles) written by Michael to help you understand the relationship
between a BIG-IP and Azure.
Cause
So how do you get BIG-IPs that are normally setup as Standalone to sync?
If you were to do each one manually over time it is likely misconfigurations between the 2 load balancers will occur.
There is also the appearance of HA/sync working or not working based on how it is configured, but for all intents and
purposes it is. How is that possible?
Recommended Actions
Four possible actions:
1. Multiple standalone appliances
Scaling out horizontally can be a matter of simple automation and there is no cluster configuration
to maintain. The only limit to the number of BIG-IP's will be any limits of the cloud load balancer.
The main disadvantage to this approach is the fear of misconfiguration by human operators.
2. Clustering with a sync-only group
A Sync-Only device group allows us to sync some configuration data between devices, but not fail
over configuration objects in floating traffic groups between devices, as we would in a
Sync-Failover gr

results.title: K13478: Overview of Connection and Persistence Mirroring (11.x)
results.content:
# Article K14431

Known Issue
A standby BIG-IP system may not update connection information for established mirrored connections.
This issue occurs when all of the following conditions are met:
Your BIG-IP systems, in an HA Sync-Failover device group, are set in an active/standby configuration.
You configure the BIG-IP systems to use both a primary and secondary IP address for mirroring.
You enable Connection Mirroring on a virtual server that uses a FastL4 profile.
A standby system either reboots or is added to the HA failover group while the active system processes
connections for a FastL4 virtual server.
When the mirroring connection is established between an active and a standby BIG-IP system, the standby system
intermittently fails to update the connection flow information for established FastL4 virtual server connections.
 This issue does not affect connections established after the mirroring connection has been established betweenNote:
the active and standby systems.
