Managing Failover
Introduction to failover
When you configure a Sync-Failover device group as part of device service clustering (DSC
Â®
), you
   ensure that a user-defined set of application-specific IP addresses, known as a
floating
    traffic group
, can fail over to another device in that device group if necessary. DSC
   failover gives you granular control of the specific configuration objects that you want to
   include in failover operations.
If you want to exclude certain devices on the network from participating in failover
   operations, you simply exclude them from membership in that particular Sync-Failover device group.
What triggers failover?
The BIG-IP system initiates failover of a traffic group according to any of several events that
      you define. These events fall into these categories:
System fail-safe
With
system fail-safe
, the BIG-IP system monitors various hardware components,
     as well as the heartbeat of various system services. You can configure the system to initiate
     failover whenever it detects a heartbeat failure.
Gateway fail-safe
With
gateway fail-safe
, the BIG-IP system monitors traffic between an active
      BIG-IP
Â®
system in a device group and a pool containing a gateway router.
     You can configure the system to initiate

results.title: K13985: Configuring a BIG-IP Link Controller high availability pair may fail
results.content:
# Article K14967

Topic
You should consider using this procedure under the following condition:
You want to change the IP addresses on a BIG-IP Link Controller system when moving the system from one
network to another; for example, moving the system from a test environment to a production environment.
Description
When you move a BIG-IP Link Controller system from one network to another, you should always add the new self IP
addresses to the system before removing the existing self IP addresses, and then update the configuration with the new
self IP addresses. Performing the desired change in this sequence allows the BIG-IP Link Controller system to
successfully establish the iQuery connections and properly rediscover the servers for the configuration. This applies to
BIG-IP Link Controller systems in a high availability (HA) configuration.
Note: If you delete the existing network configuration before adding the new network configuration, the iQuery
connections fail to establish and the BIG-IP Link Controller system does not function as expected.
Additionally, the BIG-IP Link Controller Configuration utility does not provide any means of modifying the server objects
in the configuration. As a result, you have to manually modify the server objects in the configuration using the TMOS
Shell ( ) utility.tmsh

that is currently used for the HA configuration need

results.title: K75303031: Configuring gateway fail-safe using the Configuration utility (13.x)
results.content:
# Article K91881543

Topic
This article applies to BIG-IP 11.x - 12.x. For information about other versions, refer to the following article:
K75303031: Configuring gateway fail-safe using the Configuration utility (13.x)
You should consider using this procedure under the following condition:
You require setting up the gateway fail-safe feature using the Configuration utility.
Description
The gateway fail-safe feature allows further redundancy between BIG-IP device group members that point to different
gateways. The gateway fail-safe feature allows each BIG-IP system to monitor the upstream gateway it uses to route
traffic. If the gateway is marked down, the BIG-IP system can fail over to the next active device to prevent further
disruption to traffic.
Before configuring the gateway fail-safe feature, you should consider the following information:
If the redundant BIG-IP systems reference the same virtual Hot Standby Router Protocol (HSRP)/Virtual Router
Redundancy Protocol (VRRP) gateway, specify the virtual router group IP address in the gateway fail-safe pool.
In most cases, the  action is the optimal gateway fail-safe action. You can use the  and Failover Reboot Restart
 actions when upstream equipment requires the BIG-IP system to drop the links to clear out their AddressAll
Resolution Protocol (ARP) cache or cause the interfaces to reset.
The gateway fail-safe feature uses gateway pools for monitoring the gateways. Therefore, you must assign the gateway
pools to the corr

results.title: K7467
results.content:
# Article K7467

F5 support engineers who work directly with customers write Support Solution and Knowledge articles, which give you
Topic
The BIG-IP system includes the option to install a FIPS hardware security module (HSM). The FIPS HSM is available in
the BIG-IP 6400, 6800, 6900, 8400, 8900, 10200, and 11000 platforms.
The HSM stores private keys on Flash RAM within the card. The amount of RAM available depends on the type of FIPS
card that is installed. The FIPS cards that ship with the BIG-IP LTM systems support the following maximum number of
private keys:
: The maximum number of SSL keys listed in the following table is only applicable to RSA 2048-bit private keys.Note
BIG-IP Platform Maximum RSA 2048-bit keys
i5820-DF, i7820-DF 10,000
10350v-F 100,000
5200, 7200, 10200, 11000 1,998 keys
6900, 8900 11,327 keys
The numbers differ when you use other SSL key types and bit length combinations. As expected, 512-bit RSA keys take
approximately half the space of 1024-bit keys, and 2048-bit RSA keys takes approximately twice as much space.
: The maximum number of SSL keys listed in the following table is only applicable to RSA 1024-bit private keys.Note
BIG-IP Platform Maximum RSA 1024-bit keys
6400, 6800 3835 keys
6400, 6800, 6900 (RoHS) 28,782 keys

Hardware HSM Setup and Administration
Setup of embedded FIPS platforms in a device
		group
You can configure a device group using two platforms from
			the same series with a FIPS hardware security module (HSM) installed in each
			unit. When setting up an embedded FIPS solution on a device group, you install
			the two systems and can connect to a serial console to remotely manage the
			systems. In the event that network access is impaired or not yet configured,
			the serial console might be the only way to access your system.
After you have set up and configured the systems, you can
			create the FIPS security domain by initializing the HSM and creating a
			security officer (SO) password. You must configure the same security domain
			name on all HSMs in the group.
Embedded HSM initialization and synchronization
		overview
After you have set up and configured your BIG-IP systems,
			you create a FIPS security domain by initializing the embedded HSM and then
			synchronizing all applicable HSMs.
Initialize the HSM in 5000/7000/10200
		platforms
You must initialize the hardware
			security module (HSM) installed in each unit before you can use it. When you
			are creating a device group using more than one FIPS platform, you initialize
			the HSM on one unit, and then initialize the HSM

The hardware security module (HSM) installed in your F5 r5000/r10000 FIPS platform is uninitialized by default. You must initialize the HSM before you can use it. This is typically a one-time operation.

results.title: K000138070: Support for 3072 or 4096 bit RSA keys on NITROX-III
results.content:
# Article K000138070

Description
BIG-IP models equipped with NITROX-III CNN35XX-NFBE do not support 4096 bit RSA keys when using old firmware
version.
Environment
BIG-IP iSeries devices with NITROX-III CNN35XX-NFBE:
BIG-IP i5820-DF (C125)
BIG-IP i7820-DF (C126)
BIG-IP i15820-DF (C120)
BIG-IP 10350v-FIPS (D112)
F5 r5920-DF (C136)
F5 r10920-DF (C137)
NITROX-III CNN35XX-NFBE firmware versions older than CNN35XX-NFBE-FW-1.1-05 for example
CNN35XX-NFBE-FW-1.0-39 or CNN35XX-NFBE-FW-1.0-52.
Cause
Old firmware version CNN35XX-NFBE-FW-1.0-39 or CNN35XX-NFBE-FW-1.0-52 does not support 4096 bit RSA keys.
Support for 4096 bit RSA keys has been implemented in firmware version CNN35XX-NFBE-FW-1.1-05
Support for 4096 bit RSA keys stored on NITROX-III CNN35XX-NFBE card has been implemented in BIG-IP software
version 17.1.0
To be able to use 4096 bit RSA keys stored on NITROX-III CNN35XX-NFBE card you need to use BIG-IP running
software version 17.1.0 or later with NITROX-III CNN35XX-NFBE firmware CNN35XX-NFBE-FW-1.1-05 or later.
Recommended Actions
Upgrade to the new firmware CNN35XX-NFBE-FW-1.1-05 or later.
Upgrade to BIG-IP version 17.1.0 or later.
New firmware for NITROX-III CNN35XX-NFBE is available with BIG-IP software version 17.1.0 or 16.1.4

results.title: K9020
results.content:
# Article K9020

F5 support engineers who work directly with customers write Support Solution and Knowledge articles, which give you
immediate access to mitigation, workaround, or troubleshooting suggestions.
Topic
The BIG-IP system supports a FIPS hardware security module (HSM). The module stores private keys used by SSL
profiles to process SSL traffic.
However, the private keys used to encrypt Web Management, Enterprise Manager, and iQuery access cannot be stored
in the HSM. The digital certificates and keys used for this communication must be accessible to processes outside of
Traffic Management Microkernel (TMM). Processes outside of TMM do not have access to information stored on the
HSM.
In BIG-IP LTM versions prior to 9.4, the Web Management interface incorrectly provided the  option forConvert to FIPS
the Web Management, Enterprise Manager, and iQuery SSL keys. The conversion of these keys failed. The Convert to
 option for these SSL keys was removed from the Web Management GUI. FIPS
F5 Product Development tracked this change as CR66272, and it was changed in BIG-IP version 9.4.0. For information
about upgrading, refer to the BIG-IP , , , , and  release notes.LTM ASM GTM Link Controller WebAccelerator

results.title: K4918: Overview of the F5 critical issue hotfix policy
results.content:
# Article K13929

Known Issue
SSL private keys that are stored in the FIPS hardware security module (HSM) are not synchronized during ConfigSync
operations.
This issue occurs when the following conditions are met:
The BIG-IP platform supports the FIPS hardware security module.
The BIG-IP systems are configured in a device group.
One or more SSL profiles reference keys are stored in the FIPS module.
You performed a ConfigSync operation.
For example, if you obtain an SSL key and import the key to the FIPS module on a device group member (LTM-1) and
then synchronize the configuration to the device group (LTM-2, LTM-3, LTM-4), the keys are missing from all of the
receiving device group members.
Impact
SSL connections may fail after failing over to a device group member with missing SSL keys.
Symptoms
As a result of this issue, you may encounter the following symptoms:
SSL keys are missing from device group members.
For example the  command does not display the expected SSL privatetmsh show /sys crypto fips private-key
keys on some device group members.
SSL connections may fail after failing over to a device group member with missing SSL keys.
Resolution
Status
F5 Product Development has assigned ID 364825 to this issue, and has confirmed that this issue exists in the products
listed in the  box. For information about releases or hotfixes that resolve this issue, refer to the followingApplies To
table:

After upgrade all keys are "use-fips none":
tmsh list ltm dns dnssec key use-fips
ltm dns dnssec key example.com_ksk {

}
 
Environment
BIG-IP DNS

DNSSEC
External HSM (netHSM)
Cause
BugID 758491
If this issue is occurring post v14.1.0, troubleshoot connectivity between BIG-IP and External HSM prior to initiating the
upgrade process. Do not uninstall the External HSM client from BIG-IP prior to software upgrade.
Otherwise re-install the HSM client post upgrade and re-establish communication between BIG-IP and External HSM,

results.title: K6499: BIG-IP platforms that support FIPS
results.content:
# Article K13836

Known Issue
Attempting to create or regenerate Domain Name System Security Extensions (DNSSEC) keys on a BIG-IP GTM system
with FIPS hardware may result in keys that are not stored in the FIPS hardware security module (HSM).
This issue occurs when either of the following conditions are met:
You attempt to create new DNSSEC Key Signing Keys (KSK) or Zone Signing Keys (ZSK)
DNSSEC KSK or ZSK created on a previous version are regenerated during a rollover period
In addition, all of the following conditions are met:
The BIG-IP platform contains the FIPS HSM
The DNSSEC keys have the FIPS option enabled
The DNSSEC key names are 15 characters or longer
This issue does not prevent the system from creating or regenerating the DNSSEC keys. However, the keys are not
stored in the FIPS HSM as expected.
Impact
The new or regenerated DNSSEC keys are not stored in the FIPS HSM and may not be protected from physical and
software attacks.
Symptoms
As a result of this issue, you may encounter the following symptoms:
The system logs error messages to the  file. The error messages appear similar to the following/var/log/ltm
examples:
mcpd[5392]: 01071392:3: Background command '/usr/bin/fipskey gen64
dnssec-_Common_This-Is-15Chars 1024' failed. The command exited with status -4.
mcpd[5392]: 010713e9:3: Key could not be parsed: public key output not in expected
format; [Key name must be less than 31 characters].
mcpd[5392]: 010713e7:3: FIPS manager key (dnssec-_Common_This-Is-15C

Topic
You can determine the model and type of your F5 hardware platform through the command line or the Configuration
utility. To do so, perform one of the following procedures:
Note: For BIG-IP versions 9.x and later, refer to .K10635: Determining the model and type of a BIG-IP appliance
Command line
To determine the type of hardware platform you are running, type the following command:
bigpipe global show | grep system_type
The system displays the platform type and identifier.
For example:
system_type Application Switch (D50)
In this example, you have a model D50 Application Switch (BIG-IP 5000).
Configuration utility
From the navigation pane, click .BIGpipe
In the  text box, type the following command:bigpipe
global show
In the resulting output, look for the  entry.system_type
For example:
system_type Application Switch (D50)
In this example, you have a model D50 Application Switch (BIG-IP 5000).

K6013: Overview of SCCP logging
results.content:
# Article K9633

Topic
Important: This article applies only to BIG-IP platforms that contain a Switch Card Control Processor (SCCP). To
determine whether a specific BIG-IP platform contains SCCP, refer to K9476: The F5 hardware/software
. The Always-On Management (AOM) subsystem does not support remote logging; therefore,compatibility matrix
this article is not applicable to BIG-IP platforms with AOM.
By design, the SCCP does not store log files locally in non-volatile storage, and the files are lost if the SCCP is shut
down or rebooted.
Since the SCCP is responsible for controlling the switch hardware, remote logging information reported by the SCCP can
assist with monitoring or troubleshooting if an issue occurs with the system.
To configure remote logging, perform the following procedure:
Important: In order to be able to send its logs to a remote server, the SCCP must have access to the network. If you
have not done this already, configure the SCCP network interface now. For more information, refer to K3753:
.Configuring the switch card control processor so that it can be accessed over the network

results.title: K000157902: Observing multiple 'recovery of LBA:1775093010 not
results.content:
# Article K000157902

Description
This article addresses the issue where BIG-IP LTM systems log repeated messages such as:
recovery of LBA:1775093010 not complete
notice pendsect[PID]: Drive: /dev/sda filesystem type: /dev/vg-db-sda/dat.log
warning pendsect[PID]: File affected: NONE
These logs indicate that the system is experiencing disk block recovery issues, which may be symptomatic of underlying
hardware or disk health problems.
Environment
BIG-IP Appliance
Local Disk
Cause
Possible disk failure.
Recommended Actions
1. Collect Diagnostic Data:
Generate a fresh QKview from the BIG-IP system.
Upload the QKview file to iHealth for analysis.
2. Create and securely store a UCS backup file before performing diagnostics or maintenance.
3. Run EUD:
Download and install the latest EUD files for your platform.
Schedule a maintenance window, as running EUD will make the device unavailable for traffic processing.
Follow instructions in F5’s EUD documentation to run tests and review results for disk errors.
Monitor System
4. If error messages stop appearing, continue monitoring closely.
Even if alerts cease, it is strongly recommended to complete EUD tests proactively.
If EUD confirms disk failure, create TAC case and upload gathered data.

results.content:
# Article K19865063

Topic
Starting in BIG-IP 13.1.0, the Traffic Management Microkernel (TMM) can perform live debugging while it passes traffic.
This feature may be useful if your TMM encounters serious problems that F5 cannot reproduce internally.
: Do not confuse TMM dynamic debugging, described in this article, with debugging using an instrumentedNote
TMM. An instrumented TMM is a special version delivered to specific customers in the form of an engineering hotfix, and
you must install it as an engineering hotfix.

results.title: K7447: OneConnect sessions can stall after the BIG-IP LTM system detaches the existing serverside flow and
results.content:
# Article K5050

Known Issue
NTLM authentication over HTTP requires a multi-packet handshake between the client and the server, and imposes a
requirement to persist the connection to the same pool member until authentication is completed. When you use the
HTTP profile with OneConnect enabled, the packets sent by the client to complete the authentication handshake may
not be sent to the same pool member. When the initially selected pool member does not receive the subsequent
handshake packets from the client once the handshake has been initiated, the server may repeatedly request
re-authentication. However, each new authentication attempt is likely to experience the same issue. As a result, the
authentication handshake fails, and the client is unable to complete the request.
Beginning with BIG-IP LTM 9.2, BIG-IP LTM will not close the server side of the connection when a pool member
responds with an HTTP 401 (Unauthorized) message. This action ensures that the connection uses the same pool
member until the authentication handshake is completed.
F5 Product Development tracked this issue as CR48426 and it was fixed in BIG-IP LTM 9.2. For information about
upgrading, refer to the BIG-IP  Release Notes.LTM
: For more information about another issue with OneConnect which may affect NTLM authentication, refer to Note
K7447: OneConnect sessions can stall after the BIG-IP LTM system detaches the existing serverside flow and
.makes the connection available for reuse
Archived - K5050: O

results.title: K72040312: The OneConnect profile may cause a TMM memory leak
results.content:
# Article K72040312

Known Issue
The BIG-IP system may experience Traffic Management Microkernel (TMM) memory leaks on standby systems.
This issue occurs when all of the following conditions are met:
A virtual server is configured with a OneConnect profile.
The BIG-IP system is in a high availability (HA) configuration with a standby system.
Mirroring is enabled.
The BIG-IP platform supports Clustered Microprocessing (CMP).
Impact
The standby BIG-IP system may be unable to process traffic in the event of an HA failover. 
Symptoms
As a result of this issue, you may encounter one or more of the following symptoms:
Standby BIG-IP systems experience high memory usage.
The affected BIG-IP systems may restart due to memory exhaustion.
Resolution
Status
F5 Product Development has assigned ID 687905 to this issue. F5 has confirmed that this issue exists in the products
listed in the  box, located in the upper-right corner of this article. For information aboutApplies to (see versions)
releases, point releases, or hotfixes that resolve this issue, refer to the following table.
Type of fix Fixes introduced in Related articles
Release 14.0.0 K2200: Most recent versions of F5 software
Point release/hotfix 12.1.3.6
13.1.1.2 K9502: BIG-IP hotfix and point release matrix
Workaround

on standby hosts

results.title: K30044380: Overview: OneConnect Status Reuse HTTP Profile Option
K85118168: OneConnect profile does not work properly if the server
results.content:
# Article K85118168

Related Content
Description
OneConnect profile does not work properly if the server actively closes the connection.
Environment
LTM virtual server 
OneConnect profile enabled
Cause
The OneConnect feature can increase network throughput by efficiently managing connections established between the
BIG-IP system and back-end pool members. The OneConnect feature utilizes HTTP keep-alive to minimize the number
of TCP connections to servers by making existing connections available for reuse by other clients.
For example, when a client initiates a new connection to a BIG-IP virtual server configured with a OneConnect profile,
the BIG-IP system parses the incoming HTTP request, selects a server based on the configured pool load-balancing
method, and establishes a new connection to the selected server only if no suitable idle connection exists. After the
client’s HTTP request is completed, the BIG-IP system keeps the existing server-side TCP connection idle and ready for
reuse by subsequent client requests.
However, if the server actively closes the TCP connection—by initiating TCP FIN or sending a TCP reset—the BIG-IP
system cannot reuse this connection. As a result, performance might degrade under certain circumstances, as the
expected benefit of connection reuse cannot be achieved.
Recommended Actions
When using the OneConnect profile on a BIG-IP virtual server, ensure the back-end server is configured to avoid actively
closing connections. Servers should honor

results.title: K14004049: Port exhaustion with oneconnect enabled
results.content:
# Article K14004049

Description
After enabling Oneconnect, the system complains about port exhaustion even when there are rather few connections.
Messages like the following one are present in the LTM logs.
crit tmm: Inet port exhaustion on x.x.x.x to x.x.x.x:x (proto 6)
Environment
Vxlan tunnel on the server-side that handles the outgoing traffic
Vlan on the server-side with cmp-hash dst-ip 
Oneconnect profile 
Cause
The "cmp-hash dst-ip" on vlans causes two different tmms to handle the OUT and the IN traffic on the server-side. This
misconfiguration is what is causing the accumulation of apparently unremovable connection flows, that in turn are likely
causing the unexpected "Inet port exhaustion" messages.

results.title: K000151124: WebSocket fails when a OneConnect profile is applied to
results.content:
# Article K000151124

Description
When upgrading an HTTP connection to WebSocket, authentication cookies or bearer tokens in headers can be lost or
misapplied—especially when OneConnect or custom iRules are active. This can result in failed handshakes or
unauthorized access to your WS backend.
Environment
BIG-IP LTM with an HTTP profile and WebSocket profile bound
OneConnect enabled on the Virtual Server
Custom iRules manipulating headers or using header-based security
Backend application relying on JSESSIONID cookie or Authorization header for WS authentication
Cause
WebSocket handshakes reuse TCP connections; OneConnect may pool and replay requests without original headers.
Custom header rewrites, HTTP compression, or ASM policies can strip or alter authentication headers before the WS
upgrade, causing the backend to reject the connection.
Recommended Actions
Below are some of the ways you can overcome the challenges of authentication persistence with websockets, these
recommendations might not work for your specific case, but they are guidelines on how to tackle this issue for various
scenarios:
Inspect tokens early and keep track of them:
when HTTP_REQUEST {
 if { [HTTP::cookie exists "JSESSIONID"] } {
  table set ws_token_[IP::client_addr] [HTTP::cookie "JSESSIONID"] 300
 }
}
Disable OneConnect for WS Paths

Many computing environments want to use a BIG-IP system to intelligently manage their HTTP traffic. An HTTP profile defines the way that you want the BIG-IP system to manage HTTP traffic.
You can use the default HTTP profile, with all of its default values, or you can create a custom HTTP profile. This particular implementation uses the default HTTP profile.

results.title: K26898044: Persistence methods available in F5 BIG-IP
results.content:
# Article K26898044

Description
When it becomes necessary to maintain a user session redirected to a specific pool member, it is needed to define and
assign a persistence method.
Environment
BIG-IP
LTM
Cause
User's requests within a single session will be load balanced among all pool members, if no persistence method is
defined and assigned to attending Virtual Server.
Recommended Actions
Select the most convenient persistence method and assign it to Virtual Server.
Depending on session type, there are several persistence methods to choose from.
These are the supported persistence methods in F5 Networks BIG-IP units:
Cookie persistence
Cookie persistence uses the HTTP cookie header to persist connections across a session. This technique prevents the
issues associated with simple persistence because the session ID is unique.
Destination address affinity persistence

results.title: K13478: Overview of connection and persistence mirroring (11.x - 12.x)
results.content:
# Article K15695

Related Content
K13478: Overview of connection and persistence mirroring (11.x - 12.x)
The  chapter of the Configuring HTTP Load Balancing with Source Address Affinity Persistence BIG-IP
 guideLocal Traffic Manager: Implementations
Topic
BIG-IP Microsoft Remote Desktop (MSRDP) persistence may not function in an environment where the destination
MSRDP servers reside in a route domain.?
Description
When Session Directory creates an encrypted Microsoft Terminal Services cookie to send to an MSRDP user, it is not
aware of the BIG-IP route domain configuration requirements, and the server IP address contained in the cookie does
not include BIG-IP centric route domain syntax. The BIG-IP system can parse Session Directory cookies, but the system
is unable to modify the contents of the encrypted cookie. The cookie is passed to the connected MSRDP user.
Subsequent requests from the MSRDP user, connecting to a virtual server configured with the MSRDP persistence
profile, present the Microsoft Terminal Services cookie and then the BIG-IP system attempts to determine the IP address
of the destination resource held in the cookie. The IP address obtained by the BIG-IP system from the MSTS cookie
does not contain route domain information and may not match the configured members of the virtual server associated
pool. When the BIG-IP system is unable to find a matching IP address, the connection is load balanced as a new
connection request.
F5 Product Development tracked thi

A strange behavior observed when source-address persistence profile is associated with a virtual server which is

results.title: K12538
results.content:
# Article K12538

Known Issue
This is the result of a known issue. The BIG-IP system may ignore valid persistence entries on platforms that support
Clustered Multi-Processing (CMP).
Note: For more information about CMP, refer to .K7751: Overview of Clustered Multi-Processing
This issue is known to occur with the following configurations:
The platform and virtual server support CMP processing.
The virtual server references an iRule or HTTP class profile that allows multiple pool selections for a single
connection.
The virtual server uses Source Address Affinity persistence, or another persistence type that stores the
persistence record in memory.
Note: Cookie and Hash persistence types are stateless persistence methods and do not utilize the BIG-IP persistence
table.
When a virtual server supports CMP and uses a persistence type that utilizes the persistence table, the BIG-IP system
shares the persistence records across all TMM instances. This behavior allows returning clients to persist to the correct
pool member, regardless of which TMM processes the request.
However, the CMP persistence lookup mechanism falsely assumes that there will be only one remote TMM persistence
lookup per connection. When the configuration contains an iRule or HTTP class profile that is configured to switch from a
server-side connection to a different pool based on traffic, the BIG-IP system may ignore persistence entries and send
the request to the wrong pool member.
F5 Product Development tracked

Known Issue
This is the result of a known issue. Persistence will fail if the timeout value for the Source Address Affinity persistence
profile is set to indefinite or greater than 2,147,483,647 seconds.
F5 Networks Product Development tracked this issue as CR47893 and it was fixed in BIG-IP LTM versions 9.1.1 and
9.2. For information about upgrading, refer to the BIG-IP  Release Notes.LTM
Workaround
If upgrading is not an immediate option, you can work around this issue by setting the timeout value between 1 and
2,147,483,647 seconds. To do so, perform the following procedure:
From the Configuration utility, click .Local Traffic
Click .Profiles
Click the  tab.Persistence
Select the Source Address Affinity profile you want to modify.
Enter a Timeout value between 1 and 2,147,483,647 on the Profile Configuration screen.
Click .Update

SSL Persistence
Overview: SSL Persistence
SSL persistence is a type of persistence that tracks SSL sessions
            using the SSL session ID, and it is a property of each individual pool. Using SSL
            persistence can be particularly important if your clients typically have translated IP
            addresses or dynamic IP addresses, such as those that Internet service providers
            typically assign. Even when the clientâs IP address changes, BIG-IP system still
            recognizes the session as being persistent based on the session ID.
You might want to use SSL persistence and source address affinity
            persistence together. In situations where an SSL session ID times out, or where a
            returning client does not provide a session ID, you might want the BIG-IP system to
            direct the client to the original node based on the clientâs IP address. As long as the
            clientâs simple persistence record has not timed out, the BIG-

results.title: K84417414: Forwarding network traffic between route domains
results.content:
# Article K000151515

F5 support engineers who work directly with customers write Support Solution and Knowledge articles, which give you
immediate access to mitigation, workaround, or troubleshooting suggestions.
Related Content
K84417414: Forwarding network traffic between route domains
Description
Traffic sent to a BIG-IP LTM Virtual IP (VIP) is not forwarded to backend pool members when the VIP and pool members
are on different VLANs or subnets. This occurs even though firewall rules allow connectivity between the relevant
networks, and SNAT is configured. Traffic reaches the VIP but does not reach the intended pool members
Environment
Product: BIG-IP LTM
Route domains in use to avoid overlapping network IP address spaces
Strict Isolation enabled on route domains
Cause
Strict Isolation was enabled on one or more route domains. When Strict Isolation is active, it prevents traffic from
crossing route domain boundaries, which blocks forwarding of VIP traffic to pool members located in different route
domains.
Recommended Actions
To permit cross-route domain traffic, disable Strict Isolation on both source and destination route domains. 
Additional Information
None

Different VLAN/Route Domain

results.content:
# Article K12969

Topic
This article applies to BIG-IP GTM 10.2.1 HF1 and 10.2.2. For information about other versions, refer to the following
articles:
K9655: Overview of the virtual server and link auto-discovery features (9.x - 10.x)
K13994: Configuring virtual server and link auto-discovery (11.x - 12.x)
Purpose
Use the procedures in this article to configure the BIG-IP GTM system to discover and monitor virtual server objects that
reside in non-default route domains.
Prerequisites
The BIG-IP configurations should not include route domains with overlapping IP addresses.
The BIG-IP LTM system must contain all of the following configuration elements:
 
Virtual Local Area Networks (VLANs) through which route domain traffic passes
Default and non-default route domains that represent each network segment
Self IP addresses that represent the address spaces for the route domains
Virtual servers that are associated with the route domains
Description
Route domains is a BIG-IP LTM feature that allows you to segment network traffic for different applications on the
network. Traffic management objects, such as virtual servers, can be designated to reside in the default route domain
(route-domain 0), or a non-default route domain, and process traffic for the application in its own route domain.
Starting in BIG-IP GTM 10.2.2, (BIG-IP GTM 10.2.1 HF1 also includes this functionality), it is possible to configure the
BIG-IP GTM system to discover and monitor BIG-IP virtual server objects

results.title: Configuring BIG-IP DNS on a Network with Multiple Route Domains
results.content:
Updated Date
: 01/27/2026
Configuring BIG-IP DNS on a Network with Multiple Route Domains
Overview: How do I deploy BIG-IP DNS on a network with multiple route domains?
You can deploy DNS (formerly GTM) on a network where BIG-IP Local Traffic Manager (LTM) systems are configured with multiple route domains and overlapping IP addresses.
Important:
On a BIG-IP system that includes both Local Traffic Manager LTM) and Global Traffic Manager (now BIG-IP DNS), all IP addresses that BIG-IP DNS references (virtual IP addresses, link addresses, and so on) must be associated with route domain 0.
The following figure shows BIG-IP DNS deployed in a network with multiple BIG-IP LTM systems, one configured with the default route domain (zero), and one configured with two additional route domains. BIG-IP DNS can monitor the Application1 and Application2 servers that have overlapping IP addresses and reside in different route domains. The firewalls perform the required address translation between the BIG-IP DNS and BIG-IP LTM addresses; you must configure the firewalls to segment traffic and avoid improperly routing packets between route domain 1 and route domain 2.
Before BIG-IP DNS can gather status and statistics for the virtual servers hosted on BIG-IP LTM systems that are configured with route domains, you must configure the following on each BIG-IP LTM that handles traffic for route domains:
VLANs through which traffic for your route domains passes
Route domains that represent each net

results.content:

:
Services
Applies To:
Show
Versions
F5 SSL Orchestrator
17.1.0
Services
The Service screen allows you to create services such as HTTP, ICAP,
			Layer 2 and Layer 3 inline, receive only TAP, and F5 services.
Inline
					HTTP
: You can configure inline HTTP explicit or transparent proxy
				settings with SSL Orchestrator configured as either an explicit or transparent proxy
				for extended SSL visibility and existing or new deployments. Using SSL Orchestrator,
				you can support multiple explicit and transparent proxy configurations such as: SSLO
				Explicit proxy with in-line explicit proxy as a service (EP-EP); SSLO Transparent
				proxy with in-line explicit proxy as a service (TP-EP); SSLO Explicit proxy with
				in-line transparent proxy as a service (EP-TP); SSLO Transparent proxy with in-line
				transparent proxy as a service (TP-TP).
ICAP
:
				Each ICAP service uses the Internet Content Adaptation Protocol (ICAP) RFC3507
				protocol to refer HTTP traffic to one or more Content Adaptation devices for
				inspection and possible modification. You can configure the ICAP services that are a
				part of this configuration.
Layer 2 and Layer
					3 inline
: Inline services pass traffic through one or more service
				devices at Layer 2 (LAN) or Layer 3 (IP). Each service device communicates with the
				BIG-IP device on the ingress side over two VLANs called Inward and Outward that
				carry traffic toward the intranet and the Internet, respectively.
TAP

results.title: K73741691: SSLO FTPS does not decrypt data channel
results.content:
# Article K73741691

F5 support engineers who work directly with customers write Support Solution and Knowledge articles, which give you
immediate access to mitigation, workaround, or troubleshooting suggestions.
Description
When using SSLO to send decrypted FTPS (secure ftp) via external inspection device (ie: IPS, ICAP, virus scanner),
only the control channel is sent for inspection.
Environment
BIG-IP configured via SSLO with listener for FTPS (normally port 990) with plain text inspection device and FTP
profile.
Cause
Configuration error
Recommended Actions
The FTP profile requires "ftps-mode allow" in order for SSLO to read the FTP request specifying the port to use and
open the appropriate pinholes for the traffic to pass. When using passive mode, this is the PASV command. 
In order for the decrypted data channel traffic to be sent to the inspection device, you will need to configure
"inherit-parent-profile enabled".
The ftp profile should be
ltm profile ftp ftp-via-sslo {
    allow-active-mode disabled
    app-service none
    defaults-from ftp
    ftps-mode allow
    inherit-parent-profile enabled
}
Additional Information
None

results.title: K411: Overview of packet tracing with the tcpdump utility
K49375773: tcpdump limitations on BIG-IP VIPRION systems .
results.content:
# Article K000132760

Description
Traffic flows to a vCMP guest via its host. Sometimes, as part of troubleshooting, it may be necessary to perform
concurrent packet captures on the guest and host to verify that the host passes traffic correctly to the guest.
Environment
BIG-IP
vCMP guest
vCMP host
Cause
You have a need to troubleshoot traffic flow between vCMP guest and its host.
Recommended Actions
tcpdump command to run on the guest:
# tcpdump -s0 -ni 0.0:nnn -w <filename.pcap> <filter>
tcpdump command to run on the host:
# tcpdump -s0 -ni <physical-interface> -w <filename.pcap> <filter>
Note: When capturing on a physical interface, the :nnn option should  be used. Additionally, if the host is a VIPRIONnot
system, you need to run the command on the blade where the physical interface resides. For more information, refer to 
K49375773: tcpdump limitations on BIG-IP VIPRION systems .
Example
A vCMP guest is showing "Disconnected" sync status. You would like to capture configsync traffic on the guest and its
host to verify that configsync traffic is flowing normally. The guest uses self IP   for configsync. Besides192.168.0.1
capturing traffic for the self IP, it will be useful to also capture  and  so that we can check if there is an ARPICMP ARP
issue. The filter we will use in this case is "host 192.168.0.1 or icmp or arp".
tcpdump command to run on the guest:
K000132760: How to perform concurrent packet capture on vCMP
guest and host

results.title: K20233108: Running the tcpdump utility using the p interface modifier
K00463541: A breakdown of the tcpdump utility, recommended
results.content:
# Article K00463541

Description
Tcpdump is one of the most important tools used in networking. We use it to find out why pool members are being
marked down, to validate the flow of traffic on the full proxy architecture of the BIG-IP, to validate proper SSL
handshakes, and so many other networking questions. While the logs may give us an idea or direction, when there is a
connectivity question, the tcpdump can unmask or point us in the direction needed for resolution.
However, using it is getting ever more complicated. Gone are the days of "run tcpdump and look at that TCP 3-way
Handshake" or just running its companion 'ssldump' and seeing the answer quickly. With the ever-changing landscape of
networking gathering the data to troubleshoot, or to simply verify traffic, is necessarily harder. For instance, do you know
how or if tcpdump will work with HTTP/2 or TLS1.3? How do you get a decrypted capture, with all of the security
concerns, so you can investigate layer 7 issues?
In this article not all the answers to the myriad of different "what if's" can or will be answered. But hopefully it will help you
find your way or at least provide a good starting point.
What factors will influence how effective tcpdump is for you?
You run a tcpdump but the data you expect to see is not there or it may not work at all.
Environment
You need to investigate a pool, virtual server, or routing protocol that does not appear to be functioning as
expected.
Virtual servers, pool members, nodes,

K000159071: Manually created snatpool in AS3 Tenant refered in
results.content:
# Article K000159071

1.  
Description
When deploying an AS3 declaration to a manually created partition with a pre-existing SNAT pool, the declaration failed
with error "01070320:3: Snatpool /example_app/example-snat-pool is still referenced by a virtual server."
 
Environment
AS3
Manually created partition with route domain
SNAT pool manually configured in partition
AS3 per-app deployment
 
Cause
AS3's design principle is to manage partitions declaratively based solely on what is defined in the declaration. When AS3
processes a declaration for a partition, it removes all existing objects in that partition and recreates only those specified
in the declaration. The manually created SNAT pool was being deleted during this process, causing the error when AS3
attempted to reference it. This is expected AS3 behavior - objects managed by AS3 must be declared within AS3, not
created manually via tmsh or GUI.
 
Recommended Actions
Create a "Shared" application within the tenant that declares the SNAT pool as a shared resource
Example implementation:
First, POST a declaration creating the shared SNAT pool in tenant example_app:
"example_app": {
"class": "Tenant",

results.title: K10090418: Configuring an HTTP virtual server to redirect to HTTPS
K7910
results.content:
# Article K7910

Known Issue
This is the result of a known issue. Attempting to manipulate an HTTP request after a response is sent using an iRule
may cause TMM to stop responding. This behavior typically occurs within iRules that contain two or more HTTP
commands that respond or redirect, or two separate iRules that contain HTTP commands that respond or redirect.
In the following iRule example, the  command is used to respond with a redirect toHTTP::redirect
 when an HTTP request is received:http://www.somesite.com
when HTTP_REQUEST {
HTTP::redirect "http://www.somesite.com"
if {[HTTP::uri] starts_with "/"} {
HTTP::redirect "http://www.thisothersite.com"
}
}
After the redirect is issued, the iRule moves to the next sequential command, and attempts to request the URI of the
HTTP request. However, the HTTP request no longer exists since the  command already responded toHTTP::redirect
the request. Because the request is no longer available for processing, TMM may crash.
This behavior can also occur if two iRules are associated with a virtual server and each iRule is configured to evaluate
the incoming data and then manipulate the data using an HTTP command. The first iRule will evaluate the request, and
the request will not be available when the second iRule attempts to evaluate the request.
F5 Product Development is tracking this issue as CR80714.
This issue does not affect the BIG-IP LTM 9.4.x software branch.

results.title: K14996: Configuring a virtual server to automatically redirect HTTP requests to HTTPS (11.4.0 and later)
results.content:
# Article K3847

1.  
2.  
3.  
4.  
5.  
6.  
Topic
This article applies to BIG-IP 9.0.0 - 9.3.1. For information about other versions, refer to the following articles:
K14996: Configuring a virtual server to automatically redirect HTTP requests to HTTPS (11.4.0 and later)
K7125: Configuring a virtual server to automatically redirect HTTP requests to HTTPS (9.4.0 - 11.3.0)
You can use the following rule to automatically redirect users to HTTPS when they connect to a virtual server using
HTTP:
rule redirect_rule { when HTTP_REQUEST { 
HTTP::redirect "https://[getfield [HTTP::host] ":" 1][HTTP::uri]"
}}
Note: The  function in the above iRule extracts the hostname from the host header and, if a[getfield [HTTP::host] ":" 1]
non-standard port is specified, removes the port from the host header.
You must adhere to the following requirements for this rule to work properly:
The rule must be enabled for use with the HTTP virtual server.
Virtual servers must exist for both the HTTP and HTTPS ports.
The HTTP and HTTPS virtual servers must have the same IP address.
Creating the rule using the BIG-IP LTM Configuration utility
You can create the rule by performing the following procedure:
Log in to the BIG-IP LTM Configuration utility.
Click .Local Traffic
Click .iRules
Click .Create
In the  box, type a name for the iRule.Name
For example:
redirect_rule

results.title: Bug ID 583957: The TMM may hang handling pipelined HTTP requests with certain iRule commands.
results.content:
F5 Bug ID 583957: The TMM may hang handling pipelined HTTP requests with certain iRule commands.

Severity: 3-Major | Opened: Mar 29, 2016 | Fixed In: 13.0.0, 12.1.2, 11.6.1 HF1, 11.5.4 HF3 | Affected Versions: 11.5.1, 11.5.1 HF1, 11.5.1 HF2, 11.5.1 HF3, 11.5.1 HF4, 11.5.1 HF5, 11.5.1 HF6, 11.5.1 HF7, 11.5.1 HF8, 11.5.1 HF9, 11.5.1 HF10, 11.5.1 HF11, 11.5.2, 11.5.2 HF1, 11.5.3, 11.5.3 HF1, 11.5.3 HF2, 11.5.4, 11.5.4 HF1, 11.5.4 HF2, 11.6.0, 11.6.0 HF1, 11.6.0 HF2, 11.6.0 HF3, 11.6.0 HF4, 11.6.0 HF5, 11.6.0 HF6, 11.6.0 HF7, 11.6.0 HF8, 11.6.1, 12.0.0, 12.0.0 HF1, 12.0.0 HF2, 12.0.0 HF3, 12.0.0 HF4, 12.1.0, 12.1.0 HF1, 12.1.0 HF2, 12.1.1, 12.1.1 HF1, 12.1.1 HF2

Symptoms: Rarely, the TMM may hang during a HTTP::respond or HTTP::redirect iRule command if it is part of a pipelined HTTP request.

Conditions: -- A HTTP::respond or HTTP::redirect iRule is used. -- The iRule command is in an event triggered on the client-side. -- A pipelined HTTP request is being handled.

Impact: The TMM will be restarted by SOD. Traffic disrupted while tmm restarts.

Workaround: None.

Fix Information: The TMM no longer hangs in rare situations when processing a pipelined HTTP request and invoking a HTTP::respond or HTTP::redirect iRule command.

Related articles: K10134038

Data scraped: 2026-06-27

results.title: K11143
results.content:
# Article K11143

Known Issue
Using the command or the command in a server-side iRule event may cause theHTTP::respond HTTP::redirect 
BIG-IP ASM or BIG-IP WebAccelerator to abort the connection by sending a TCP RST to the client system instead of
forwarding the expected response. In addition, this issue may cause theTMM process to dump core with a SIGABRT. 
For example, using the following iRule with an BIG-IP ASM or BIG-IP WebAccelerator-enabled virtual server causes the
BIG-IP ASM or BIG-IP WebAccelerator system to abort the connection by sending a TCP RST to the client, and may
cause TMM crash: to 
when HTTP_RESPONSE {
  if { [HTTP::status] == 200 } {
    HTTP::respond 302 Location "http://www.example.com/"
  }
}
F5 Product Development tracked this issue as ID 221949 (formerly CR103446) and it was fixed in version 11.0.0. For
information about upgrading, refer to the BIG-IP  or  release notes.ASM WebAccelerator
Workaround
You can work around this issue by performing one of the following procedures:
Using the  command instead of the  commandHTTP::header replace HTTP::respond 
Disabling BIG-IP ASM or BIG-IP WebAccelerator processing in the iRule event before the  or HTTP::respond
 commandHTTP::redirect
Using the HTTP::header replace command instead of HTTP::respond
You may also be able to work around this issue by using the  command instead of the HTTP::header replace
 command.HTTP::respond

command in a server-sid

results.content:
# Article K15659

This topic is divided into four articles. This article covers BIG-IP 11.6.0 HF6. The other three articles cover the following
hotfixes:
BIG-IP 11.6.0 HF5
BIG-IP 11.6.0 HF4
BIG-IP 11.6.0 HF3, H2, H1
F5 has recently discovered and corrected a number of issues that affect customers running ARX 6.3.0. F5 recommends
that all customers currently running ARX 6.3.0 install the latest cumulative rollup hotfix available.
The following table lists the rollup hotfixes available for ARX 6.3.0. Each hotfix lists the ID number and description for the
issues fixed by the hotfix:
: These rollup hotfixes are cumulative; each hotfix contains the fixes included in the previous hotfixes.Note
BIG-IP
Version     
ID
Number Description
473033 Datastor Now Uses Syslog-ng
507312 icrd segmentation fault
544980 Small /var when deploying from OVF for BETTER and BEST
520466 Ability to edit iCall scripts is removed from resource administrator role
527630 CVE-2015-1788 : OpenSSL Vulnerability

results.title: K000092982: iRule command SSL::enable / SSL::Disable is applied the
results.content:
# Article K000092982

Description
When using 'SSL::enable' or 'SSL::disable ' command in iRule it is important to pay attention to the context in which it is
applied.
 
 
Environment
BIG-IP LTM
iRules SSL events
 
Cause
iRule command SSL::enable / SSL::Disable is applied the context of the event in which it is called
 
Recommended Actions
For instance., the following iRule would cause the SSL session to be reset upon matching host name.
ltm rule iRule_Hostname {
 
when HTTP_REQUEST {
log local0. "logging on"
 
if {[HTTP::host] equals "example.com"} {
SSL::disable
persist source_addr
pool Pool_example
}
K000092982: iRule command SSL::enable / SSL::Disable is applied the
context of the event in which it is called
https://my.f5.com/manage/s/article/K000092982
 Published Date: Jan 11, 2023 UTC Updated Date: Jan 11, 2023 UTC

F5 support engineers who work directly with customers write Support Solution and Knowledge articles, which give you
immediate access to mitigation, workaround, or troubleshooting suggestions.
Related Content
Clouddocs > > SSL
}
}
 
To achieve desirable result, either configure the iRule to specify the SSL::disable command in appropriate event or
specify the context along with the command.
 
when HTTP_REQUEST {
log local0. "logging on"
 
if {[HTTP::host] equals "example.com"} {
SSL::disable serverside
persist source_addr
pool Pool_example
}
}
 
 
Additional Information
None

results.title: K52245924: Bypassing the BIG-IP WebAccelerator or BIG-IP AAM or BIG-IP HTTP cache with an iRule in
results.content:
# Article K10127

Topic
This article applies to BIG-IP 10.x. For information about other versions, refer to the following articles:
K52245924: Bypassing the BIG-IP WebAccelerator or BIG-IP AAM or BIG-IP HTTP cache with an iRule in
11.x through 13.x
K6882: Bypassing BIG-IP WebAccelerator with an iRule in version 9.4.x
To bypass the BIG-IP WebAccelerator in 10.x, perform the following procedure as appropriate for your BIG-IP
WebAccelerator version:
Bypassing BIG-IP WebAccelerator in 10.1.0 and later
In 10.1.0 and later, you can bypass BIG-IP WebAccelerator processing based on information in either the request or the
response. To bypass BIG-IP WebAccelerator processing based on information in the request, use the HTTP::class
 iRules command in the  iRules event. To bypass BIG-IP WebAccelerator processing based ondisable HTTP_REQUEST
information in the response, use the  iRules command in the  iRules event. WAM::disable HTTP_RESPONSE
: In most cases, when bypassing BIG-IP WebAccelerator, you should also explicitly disable BIG-IP LTM RAMNote
Cache using the  iRules command to prevent the bypassed content from being cached by theCACHE::disable
BIG-IP LTM system. However, there is no need to re-enable BIG-IP WebAccelerator processing or BIG-IP LTM RAM
Cache after disabling for a single request. BIG-IP LTM RAM Cache is implicitly enabled at the start of the 
event, and BIG-IP WebAccelerator processing is implicitly enabled for each request matching theHTTP_REQUEST 
HTTP class filter.

results.title: Bug ID 574860: HTTP request dropped when using ACCESS::disable from iRule and a Per-Request Policy
results.content:
F5 Bug ID 574860: HTTP request dropped when using ACCESS::disable from iRule and a Per-Request Policy

Severity: 3-Major | Opened: Feb 19, 2016 | Fixed In: 12.1.0, 12.0.0 HF3 | Affected Versions: 11.6.0, 11.6.0 HF1, 11.6.0 HF2, 11.6.0 HF3, 11.6.0 HF4, 11.6.0 HF5, 11.6.0 HF6, 11.6.0 HF7, 11.6.0 HF8, 11.6.1, 11.6.1 HF1, 11.6.1 HF2, 11.6.2, 11.6.2 HF1, 11.6.3, 11.6.3.1, 11.6.3.2, 11.6.3.3, 11.6.3.4, 11.6.4, 11.6.5, 11.6.5.1, 11.6.5.2, 11.6.5.3, 12.0.0, 12.0.0 HF1, 12.0.0 HF2

Symptoms: When ACCESS::disable command is used in an iRule along with a Category Lookup agent in a per-request policy, the HTTP request we be incorrectly dropped and the connection reset. This error condition may also occur with other per-request policy agents.

Conditions: APM deployed with a Per-Request policy using a Category-Lookup agent and an iRule which issues the ACCESS::disable command associated on the same virtual server.

Impact: The HTTP request will be dropped or the HTTP connection will stall and timeout.

Fix Information: When ACCESS::disable is used in an iRule on a virtual server with an Access Profile and Per-Request Policy assigned, BIG-IP APM will not run the Per-Request policy.

Related articles: K10134038

Data scraped: 2026-06-27

# Article K7920

Topic
Note: For more information, refer to .K10479: Bypassing the BIG-IP ASM system with an iRule in 10.x
In BIG-IP ASM 9.4.0 and 9.4.1, to bypass application security inspection you had to set the  flag within anasm_bypass
iRule:
To disable ASM
set asm_bypass 1
To enable ASM
set asm_bypass 0
Note: For an example iRule using the  command, refer to asm_bypass K7616: Configuring static web content to
.bypass the BIG-IP ASM
In BIG-IP ASM 9.4.2 through 9.4.8, the following iRule commands are used to enable or disable BIG-IP ASM processing
for certain requests or connections:
To disable ASM
PLUGIN::disable ASM
To enable ASM
PLUGIN::enable ASM
Note: When you use the command to bypass the BIG-IP ASM system for one request, you shouldPLUGIN::disable 
always re-enable ASM processing for subsequent connections by using the command at the top of the PLUGIN::enable 
 event. Otherwise, all subsequent requests on the connection will bypass the BIG-IP ASMHTTP_CLASS_SELECTED

K000148734: iRule comments will not disable elseif statements
results.content:
# Article K000148734

Description
Using  to comment out an  clause in a  structure will not prevent the  from executing, or may# elseif if/elseif/else elseif
result in a syntax error.
Take the following TCL code:
set my_var 0
set chk {1}
if {$chk eq "0" } {
   set my_var 1<
   break
#} elseif {$chk eq "1" } {
#    set my_var 1
#    break
} elseif {$chk eq "1" } {
   set my_var 2
  break
}
With the comments ( ) in place as they are, we might expect that when executed, the second elseif stanza will run, and #
 has a final value of my_var 2
However, it actually holds a value of 0
The first (apparently commented)   clauseelseif
elseif {$chk eq "1" }
is evaluated and selected, but no variables are set due to the comments,
#    set my_var 1
#    break
and the following   clauseelseif
elseif {$chk eq "1" }
is not evaluated.

results.title: K15573541: Disabling a violation for a URL using an iRule
results.content:
# Article K15573541

Topic
You should consider using this procedure under the following condition:
You want to disable a specific BIG-IP ASM violation for a URL.
Description
In some cases, you may want to override the security policy blocking action for a specific violation and forward the
request to the origin server (OWS). For example, while building the security policy, you may want to disable a specific
BIG-IP ASM violation for a URL while leaving the violation enabled for other URLs. To do so, you can use the 
iRules command.ASM::unblock 
The following applies to using the  iRules command: ASM::unblock
Violations from an unblocked request do not generate learning suggestions.
The command applies to requests only.
The command does not apply to requests that are part of attacks such as web scraping or brute force attacks.
The command takes effect even if it is followed by an iRules command with a blocking violation.
Prerequisites
You must meet the following prerequisites to use this procedure:
You have access to the BIG-IP ASM Configuration utility.
You have identified the specific violation that you want to forward to the OWS.
Procedures
Enable BIG-IP ASM iRules events
Determine the violation name
Define the iRule to disable a specific violation for a URL
Enable BIG-IP ASM iRules events
Before you configure the iRule to disable a specific violation for a URL, you must enable ASM iRules events for the
security policy. To do so, perform one of the following procedures, depe

results.title: K000092906: Traffic not forwarded to pool members when DOSL7/Bot
results.content:
# Article K000092906

Description
Traffic may appear to be blackholed and not forwarded to pool members when DOSL7::disable or
BOTDEFENSE::disable iRule commands are used together with HTTP::disable command.
 
Example 1:
ltm rule rule_bot_disable {
when CLIENT_ACCEPTED {
        HTTP::disable
        BOTDEFENSE::disable
    }
}
Example 2:
ltm rule rule_dosl7_disable {
    when CLIENT_ACCEPTED {
        HTTP::disable
        DOSL7::disable
    }
}
Environment
BIG-IP ASM / Advanced WAF
DoSL7 or Bot Defense Profiles attached to Virtual Server
iRule using commands to disable DOSL7 (DOSL7::disable) or Bot Defense (BOTDEFENSE::disable)
iRule using command to disable HTTP (HTTP::disable)
 
Cause
Bug ID 1110281 and Bug ID 974985
 
Recommended Actions
K000092906: Traffic not forwarded to pool members when DOSL7/Bot
Defense and HTTP are disabled in iRule

results.title: K65408955: Disabling an attack signature for a URL using an iRule
results.content:
# Article K65408955

Topic
You should consider using this procedure under the following condition:
You want to disable an attack signature for a URL using an iRule.
Description
In some cases you may want to disable an attack signature for a URL and forward the request to the origin web server
(OWS). For example, while building the security policy, you may want to disable a specific attack signature for a URL
while leaving the signature enabled for other URLs. To do so, you can use the  iRules command.ASM::unblock

information, refer to .K8866: Disabling attack signature checks for specific objects
The following applies to using the  iRules command:ASM::unblock
Violations from an unblocked request do not generate learning suggestions.
The  iRules command applies only to requests.ASM::unblock

results.content:
Manual Chapter
:
Per-Request Policy Item Reference
Applies To:
Show
Versions
BIG-IP APM
21.0.0
,
17.1.3
,
17.1.2
,
17.1.1

17.0.0
,
16.1.6

16.1.5

,
16.1.1
,
16.1.0
Per-Request Policy Item Reference
About per-request policy items
When configuring a per-request policy, many agents are available for inclusion in the per-request policy.
About SSL check
Specifies that the system performs an SSL check, which determines whether SSL
            is found on the endpoint. It provides two default branches: SSL Found and fallback.
            There is no configuration required for this action.
About per-request policy authentication items
Authentication items perform authentication or authentication-related
   functions.
About AD Group Lookup
An AD Group Lookup item can branch based on Active Directory group. The item provides one default advanced branch rule expression,
expr
{ [
mcget
{
session.ad.last.attr.primaryGroupID
}] ==
100
}
, as an example.
A branch rule expression can include any populated session variable, such as
session.ad.last.attr.primaryGroupID
,
session.ad.last.attrmemberOf
,
session.ad.last.attr.lastLogon
,
session.ad.last.attr.groupType
,
session.ad.last.attr.member
, and so on. As an example,
expr
{ [
mcget
{
session.ad.last.attr.memberOf
}] contains
"CN=Administrators"
is a valid expression.
An AD Query action can populate the session variables.
About LDAP Group
  Lookup
An LDAP Group Lookup item compares a specified string against the
session.ldap.last

# Article K13343

Topic
F5 has recently discovered and corrected a number of issues that affect customers running BIG-IP 11.1.0. F5
recommends that all customers currently running 11.1.0 install the latest cumulative rollup hotfix.
The following table lists the rollup hotfixes released for BIG-IP 11.1.0. The table lists each hotfix, along with the ID
numbers of issues that the corresponding hotfix resolves, and a description of each issue. If an article exists for the

results.title: Bug ID 1154533: Deleting a data-group in use by an iRule does not trigger a validation error when using matchclass
results.content:
F5 Bug ID 1154533: Deleting a data-group in use by an iRule does not trigger a validation error when using matchclass

Severity: 4-Minor | Opened: Sep 13, 2022 | Affected Versions: 14.1.4, 14.1.4.1, 14.1.4.2, 14.1.4.3, 14.1.4.4, 14.1.4.5, 14.1.4.6, 14.1.5, 14.1.5.1, 14.1.5.2, 14.1.5.3, 14.1.5.4, 14.1.5.6

Symptoms: Log files /var/log/tmm reports an error about invalid matchclass, for example: localhost err tmm[17817]: 01220001:3: TCL error: /Common/gen-snat <CLIENT_ACCEPTED> - invalid matchclass operands - no class or list type found, lhs: ip, rhs: /Common/mydatagroup (line 15) invoked from within "matchclass [IP::local_addr] equals /Common/mydatagroup"

Conditions: -- Delete a data group. -- iRule with keyword matchclass uses that data-group on a virtual server For example: if { [matchclass [IP::local_addr] equals /Common/mydatagroup] } {

Impact: The in-use data-group can be deleted without error. iRule aborts lead to connection issues.

Workaround: Replace obsoleted "matchclass" keyword with "class match"

Related articles: K10134038

Data scraped: 2026-06-27

results.title: K3386
results.content:
# Article K3386

Topic
: This article applies only to version 9.x iRules, and assumes that you are familiar with assigning an iRule to a virtualNote
server. For detailed information about iRules and data groups for BIG-IP version 9.x, refer to the  chapterWriting iRules
of the Configuration Guide for Local Traffic Management. For detailed information about iRules and data groups for
.BIG-IP version 10.x, refer to the DevCentral iRules wiki
: For information about how to locate F5 product guides, refer to Note K12453464: Finding product documentation on
.AskF5
Beginning with version 9.0.0, data groups replace the class lists that are found in BIG-IP 4.x. Data groups are a
collection of related elements, such as a set of IP addresses for AOL clients, and are useful with iRules. For example,
you can use a data group to separate traffic based on URI components.
Data Group Types
Data groups can be one of the following types:
Address:
An address data group consists of a collection of IP addresses.
String:
A string address group consists of a group of strings, such as *.jpg.
Integer:
An integer data group consists of a group of integers.

results.title: K8804
results.content:
# Article K8804

Known Issue
This is the result of a known issue. The  iRule command searches a data group list for a member that startsfindclass
with a specified string, and returns the data group member string. Using the  iRule command to search a datafindclass
group containing IP addresses may cause TMM to crash.
For example, the following iRule references a Host data group called , containing an IP address of :hostclass 10.1.1.1
when CLIENT_ACCEPTED {      set fc [findclass [client_addr] $::hostclass]      log
local0. "findclass found $fc"}class hostclass {   host 10.1.1.1}
When the BIG-IP processes a connection that traverses the iRule, TMM may crash.
F5 Product Development tracked this issue as CR93913 and it was fixed in BIG-IP version 9.4.5. For information about
upgrading, refer to the BIG-IP  release notes.LTM
Workaround
To work around this issue, use the  iRule command to perform a comparison against a data group.matchclass
For example:
when CLIENT_ACCEPTED {  if { [matchclass [IP::client_addr] equals $::hostclass] } {     
pool http_pool  }}
Archived - K8804: Using the findclass iRule command to search a data
group containing IP addresses may cause TMM to crash

Verify that the file types listed represent the static image files on the web server.
Click .Update
Creating a static web content pool
Log in to the Configuration utility.
Click .Local Traffic
Click .Pools
Click .Create
Type a name for the pool.
For example:
static_content_pool
Choose a monitor.

results.title: K10745
results.content:
# Article K10745

Topic
Data Group names containing a special character must be enclosed with brace characters ( ) when referenced in an{ } 
iRule. If the Data Group name contains a special character such as a period (  ), or a hyphen ( ) and no brace.  - 
characters are used around the name in the iRule, the Tool Command Language (Tcl) misinterprets the Data Group
name when a connection triggers the iRule.
: An underscore character (  ) does not require brace characters when referenced in an iRule.Note  _
For example, the following Data Group, , contains a period in the name:test.network  
class test.network {
   network 172.16.64.0/24
}

containing special characters

results.title: Bug ID 1041261: iRule sideband connection might use a local MAC address for a floating IP address
K43093123: "Connect" or "Send" command in iRule, might cause
results.content:
# Article K43093123

Description
 
Sometimes it is required to start a connection from an iRule, but when doing this operation, BIG-IP
unit might use a wrong mac address.
Due to behavior above, Cisco ACI might "blacklist" BIG-IP's floating ip address.
 
 
Environment
 
BIG-IP
Mac masquerade
iRule(s) containing "Connect" or "Send" commands.
 
 
 
Cause
 
This behavior is caused by ID 1041261:
 
"When MAC masquerade is in use and sideband connection is generated with iRule (using
'connect' and 'send' commands) and floating self-ip is used as a source of the connection, the
"outgoing sideband packets are not using the masquerade MAC address.
 
K43093123: "Connect" or "Send" command in iRule, might cause
wrong mac address to be used.
https://my.f5.com/manage/s/article/K43093123
 Published Date: Mar 06, 2025 UTC Updated Date: Mar 06, 2025 UTC
Applies to 
 BIG-IP
 BIG-IP Platforms
 VIPRION Platforms
 Virtual Editions

Cisco ACI might blacklist floating ip address due to "Rogue Endpoint Detected" feature.
 
 
Recommended Actions
 
Instead of specifying a source address in iRule, direct traffic (from same iRule) to specially
configured virtual server (with no VLAN access):
 
 
Procedure
 
Modify iRule to send connection to local VS:
 
When CLIENT_ACCEPTED { 
 set data "MyData" 
 #set conn_id [connect -protocol UDP -myaddr aaa.bbb.ccc.ddd  -myport <port> eee.fff.ggg.hhh:80]   <<<<<< Original
"Connect" command
 set conn_id [connect -protocol UDP sideband_vs]

results.title: Bug ID 513319: Incorrect of failing sideband connections from within iRule may leak memory
results.content:
F5 Bug ID 513319: Incorrect of failing sideband connections from within iRule may leak memory

Severity: 3-Major | Opened: Mar 19, 2015 | Fixed In: 12.0.0, 11.6.1 HF1, 11.5.4 | Affected Versions: 11.5.1, 11.5.1 HF1, 11.5.1 HF2, 11.5.1 HF3, 11.5.1 HF4, 11.5.1 HF5, 11.5.1 HF6, 11.5.1 HF7, 11.5.1 HF8, 11.5.1 HF9, 11.5.1 HF10, 11.5.1 HF11, 11.5.2, 11.5.2 HF1, 11.5.3, 11.5.3 HF1, 11.5.3 HF2, 11.6.0, 11.6.0 HF1, 11.6.0 HF2, 11.6.0 HF3, 11.6.0 HF4, 11.6.0 HF5, 11.6.0 HF6, 11.6.0 HF7, 11.6.0 HF8, 11.6.1

Symptoms: When using sideband connections within iRules, the internal TMM memory structures might leak if the sideband destination is not reachable (routing, etc.).

Conditions: Unreachable sideband destination that lead to failures of the sideband connection creation, e.g. destination is not reachable via routing.

Impact: Gradual memory usage in TMM, which can lead to aggressive memory sweeper and eventual failover/outage. This might manifest in gradual increment of TMM memory usage in graphs, particularly, the following: -- High number of connfails in tmctl sb_stats. -- High number of allocated memory in tmctl sb_cache.

Workaround: Correct possible reachability issues to the sideband destination.

Fix Information: TMM no longer leaks memory when the sideband destination is unreachable.

Related articles: K10134038, K17078

Data scraped: 2026-06-27

results.content:
# Article K000136016

F5 support engineers who work directly with customers write Support Solution and Knowledge articles, which give you
immediate access to mitigation, workaround, or troubleshooting suggestions.
Description
You receive an error in the configuration utility that some fields of the new iRule are incorrect. After checking the LTM
logs, you see the following error message:
010716ac:3: /Common/HSSR::http_req(rule::procedure) cannot be found.
Note: The HSSR iRule implements a nifty HTTP client 'http_req' using SIDEBAND. It handles challenges like redirects
and cookies (lets you access APM- protected services!), IPv6, chunked transfer-coding, easy HTML forms, and Basic
AuthN.
 For more information regarding SIDEBAND connections refer to the SIDEBAND article on F5 Cloud Docs.Note:
Environment
BIG-IP LTM
iRule using SIDEBAND
iRule that attempts to create a sideband connection using http_req
Cause
Missing iRule HSSR in the configuration.
Recommended Actions
Create or copy the HSSR iRule in use on the previous device to the new device, in this way the SIDEBAND iRule will call
it. 
Additional Information
HTTP Super SIDEBAND Requestor (Client) Handles Redirects, Cookies, Chunked Transfer, APM Access, etc

# Article K14406

Known Issue
iRules that use sideband connections may leak memory.
This issue occurs when all of the following conditions are met:
An iRule uses a sideband connection command ( ).connect
For example:
when CLIENT_ACCEPTED {
    set conn_id [connect 10.10.10.10:80]

The virtual server associates with the iRule.
The client is connected to the virtual server.
Each time an iRule processes the  command, the amount of memory allocated to the Traffic Managementconnect
Microkernel (TMM) increases. This behavior may eventually cause the TMM process to exit.

results.title: K70034058: F5OS tenant status indicates Pending
results.content:
# Article K70034058

1.  
2.  
3.  
4.  
1.  
2.  
Description
When you create a new tenant on the F5OS platforms, the system sets the default state for the tenant to Configured and
does not allocate any hardware resources (vCPU, memory, or storage) to the tenant. When you change the state of the
tenant to Provisioned, the status indicates Provisioning while the system allocates the requested hardware resources to
the tenant. It then changes to Provisioned when the allocation completes.
When you attempt to change the state of the tenant to Deployed, the system checks if the allocated resources are
available for the tenant. If there are not enough resources for the system to start and run the tenant, the status of the
tenant remains Pending until sufficient resources are available to meet the request. When sufficient resources are made
available to meet the tenant's request, the status of the tenant indicates Running and the system starts the tenant. This
behavior is by design.
You can view the status of a tenant using any of the following methods:
Use the webUI
Use the command line
Use the REST API
: Performing the following procedures should not have a negative impact on your system.Impact of procedure
Use the webUI
Log in to the partition webUI as the partition administrator for a VELOS system, or the webUI as the admin user
for an F5 rSeries system.
Go to  > .TENANT MANAGEMENT Tenant Details
For  , select the tenant name.Tenant Name

results.title: K000152629: F5OS - After upgrade tenant fail to start with error
results.content:
# Article K000152629

Description
After upgrading an F5OS appliance from version 1.7 to 1.8.0, BIG-IP tenants may fail to start with errors such as:
"Allocating resources to tenant is in progress."
"Resource allocation failed - Node is up but Platform services not responding."
Additionally, when attempting to provision a tenant, the following error may appear: "Storage size(76) must be
greater than or equal to 142"
Environment
Product: F5OS - Appliance or Velos
Version: 1.8.0 (upgrade from 1.7.0)
BIG-IP tenants using T4 disk images
Tenants originally created on F5OS-A 1.1.0 with storage size below the minimum required for T4 images
Cause
A validation bug in F5OS-A 1.1.0 allowed creation of BIG-IP tenants with T4 disk images and a storage size below the
required minimum (142 GB). This issue is documented in F5 Bug . While F5OS 1.7.0 and later enforce theID1161557
minimum disk size for newly created tenants, the validation does not retroactively check tenants created on earlier,
affected versions. As a result, after upgrading to F5OS 1.8.0, tenants with insufficient storage fail to start and display the
error: "Storage size(76) must be greater than or equal to 142".
Recommended Actions
Before upgrading F5OS, verify that all BIG-IP tenants meet the minimum required disk size for their image type.
For T4 images, the minimum is 142 GB.
If a tenant fails to start after upgrade due to insufficient storage, set the tenant to "configured" mode, increase the
storage allocation to meet

# Article K000150395

Description
When attempting to upgrade from a BIG-IP version prior to version 17.x, the configuration fails to load. Running the
command  in v17.X produces an error similar to:load sys config verify
 
Provisioning failed with error 1 - 'Disk limit exceeded. 20338 MB are required to
provision these modules, but only 20124 MB are available.'
Unexpected Error: Validating configuration process failed.
 
Environment
F5OS Tenant
Upgrade to v17.X
Disk size less than 82GB
 
Cause
The disk space requirement for F5OS tenants increased from 77GB in version 15.x to 82GB in version 17.x. Systems
with less than the required disk space fail during provisioning due to insufficient resources.
 
Recommended Actions
Verify the current disk space allocation using the command:
tmsh list sys disk
Increase disk space allocation for F5OS tenants as per documentation guidelines referenced below:
K45191957: Overview of the BIG-IP tenant image types
Follow instructions on expanding disk space provided in relevant documentation.

error 1 - 'Disk limit exceeded - F5OS

results.title: K000140117: F5OS reporting "Insufficient memory on node" message
results.content:
# Article K000140117

Description
F5OS system is reporting a message similar to the following when a running ( ) tenant is moved to Deployed
or , then back to , in a short space of a time:Provisioned Configured Deployed
Insufficient memory on node Tenant deployment will be processed when requested
memory/hugepages available
Environment
F5OS tenant
Tenant deployment
Cause
This might happen if you stop a running tenant, then attempt to deploy it again, seconds later. In this case, memory
resources haven't yet been released from the tenant's previous  pod, so kubernetes may not be able tovirt-launcher
schedule another  pod to the blade until that one was cleaned up and resources returned to the availablevirt-launcher
pool.
This would be more likely to occur if the tenant in question is configured to use a large amount of memory (or multiple
tenants are running and using a large amount of memory in total).
Recommended Actions
This message is not cause for concern, as long as it goes away without user intervention and the tenant eventually starts
up without issues.
If the message persists, there may be an issue with the system that requires further investigation to diagnose and fix.
Generate controller and partition qkviews, then open a case with F5 Support, referencing this article.
Additional Information
This message might also appear if the node (blade) has a genuine, persistent issue with available memory. In that case,
the issue won't go away on its own.
K000140117: F5OS repo

results.title: K000157374: F5OS-C VELOS Upgrade: Partition Key Corruption and
results.content:
# Article K000157374

Description
After upgrading F5 VELOS Chassis from F5OS-C version 1.6.2 to 1.8.1, customers may encounter a situation where all
tenants within a newly created or existing partition fail to deploy and remain in an INOPERATIVE state. Attempts to
create or redeploy tenants result in errors such as "Tenant deployment failed - Server is not responding." Log files may
show repeated messages like "Key header check failed" from the api-svc-gateway process. Additionally,
controller-manager pods in the kube-service-catalog namespace may enter a CrashLoopBackOff state due to expired
Service Catalog API Server certificates, resulting in further deployment failures.
Environment
F5 VELOS Chassis (e.g., F5-VEL-CX410-AC, F101)
F5OS-C software upgrade from version 1.6.2 to 1.8.1
System configuration with two system controllers and multiple blades
Partitions with ID=1 (not limited to those named "default")
Tenants deployed within affected partitions
OpenShift/Kubernetes environment with Service Catalog API Server
Service Catalog API Server certificate present and potentially expired
Actions: Upgrade, partition recreation, tenant deployment, certificate renewal
Cause
Bug ID 1642081 (partition key corruption for partitions with ID=1 during upgrade from affected versions).
 (expired Service Catalog API Server certificate causing controller-manager CrashLoopBackOff).Bug ID 1962261
Recommended Actions
If tenants are INOPERATIVE and "Key header check failed" errors are present a

Tenant Management

Tenant Management
Tenants overview
A
tenant
is a guest system running software in a chassis partition (for example, a
BIG-IP
system).
VELOS
systems support multitenancy, in that you can run several tenants in the same chassis partition, including BIG-IP and BIG-IP Next tenants. The maximum number of lightweight tenants that can be created in a blade is 22. Each blade has 128GB memory, of which 95GB is reserved for tenants. Each lightweight tenant requires a minimum of one vCPU and 4096MB minimum memory.
Information about
BIG-IP Next
is available on
my.f5.com
, on
clouddocs.f5.com
, and in the article
How to: Configure multi-tenancy for BIG-IP Next on VELOS
.
The administrator can connect to the tenantâs webUI, CLI, or REST API and have the same experience as on their existing
F5
platforms. A tenant on the
VELOS
platform is managed similarly to how a vCMP guest is managed today on the VIPRION platform. The tenant is assigned dedicated vCPU and memory resources and is restricted to specific VLANs for network connectivity.
The chassis partition administrator is responsible for configuring tenant deployments within the chassis partition. Once a tenant has been deployed, there is a per-tenant administrator role, whose responsibilities include configuring the services that are available on that tenant.
Tenants inherit certain capabilities, such as the license, VLANs, and management interface speed, from the
