results.title: Bug ID 1623325: VLAN groups or VLAN group members may be deleted on F5OS tenant
results.content:
F5 Bug ID 1623325: VLAN groups or VLAN group members may be deleted on F5OS tenant

Severity: 2-Critical | Opened: Aug 02, 2024 | Affected Versions: 15.1.8, 15.1.8.1, 15.1.8.2, 15.1.9, 15.1.9.1, 15.1.10, 15.1.10.2, 15.1.10.3, 15.1.10.4, 15.1.10.5, 15.1.10.6, 15.1.10.7, 15.1.10.8, 17.1.0, 17.1.0.1, 17.1.0.2, 17.1.0.3, 17.1.1, 17.1.1.1, 17.1.1.2, 17.1.1.3, 17.1.1.4, 17.1.2, 17.1.2.1, 17.1.2.2, 17.1.3, 17.1.3.1, 17.1.3.2, 17.5.0, 17.5.1, 17.5.1.2, 17.5.1.3, 17.5.1.4, 17.5.1.5, 17.5.1.6

Symptoms: If using VLAN groups on a tenant running on an rSeries appliance or VELOS chassis, the system may delete the VLAN group or VLAN group members unexpectedly. This will happen when configuration changes to the tenant are made in F5OS or if the interface members of the VLAN change state (i.e. link down) - If the VLAN groups are in a non-common partition, any members of the VLAN group will be removed, but the VLAN group will remain. - If the VLAN groups are in common partition, but are not referenced by higher-level objects, the VLAN group will be removed. - If the VLAN groups are in common partition and are referenced by higher-level objects, the system will not delete the VLAN group, but will log messages similar to the following: err mcpd[9181]: 01070623:3: The vlangroup (/Common/otters-vlangroup) is referenced by one or more virtual servers. err chmand[4691]: 012a0003:3: hal_mcp_process_error: result_code=0x1070623 for result_operation=eom result_type=eom

Conditions: - BIG-IP tenant run

results.title: Network Settings
results.content:
Manual Chapter
:
Network Settings
Applies To:
Show
Versions
F5OS-A
1.7.0
Network Settings
Network settings overview
An administrator can configure L2 network settings for the
rSeries
system, such as port
			groups, LAGs, interfaces, VLANs, LACP, LLDP, and STP. You can configure these
			network settings from the webUI, the CLI, or REST APIs.
Port groups overview
The front-panel ports on
F5 r2000/r4000 and F5
				r5000/r10000 platforms
support port group functionality. Port groups enable you
			to configure the mode of the physical port, which controls the port speed and whether
			the port is bundled or unbundled. Until configured, the
rSeries
system uses port speeds of
100G, 25G,
				or 10G, depending on the port and the platform
. You can change them based on
			what optical transceiver module type you are using.
F5
				r2000/r4000 platforms have pre-defined configuration modes. These port group options
				are 4x25GbE, 8x10GbE, and 4x10GbE+2x25GbE.
Before configuring any interfaces, VLANs, or LAGs, you can set up
			port groups so that physical interfaces on the
platform
are configured for the proper speed and bundling. Depending
			on the port group mode, a different FPGA version is loaded, and the speed of
			the port is adjusted accordingly. The system creates the port group
				components.
Changing the mode for a port group reboots the
system
, removes stale interfaces from your configuration, and
			removes any references to stale interfaces from your configuration. Y

Install or Upgrade Software
Installation and upgrade options
There are several types of installation and upgrade options for
F5OS
software:
System upgrade
Clean (or bare metal) installation
System upgrade
A system upgrade installs new Base OS software on the system and results in a service outage. This method preserves old image and configuration data, and includes these three sub-types:
ISO upgrade
Upgrades both the operating system (OS) and services for the Base OS software.
Partial upgrade
Upgrades OS or services for Base OS software. The OS partial update file has a .os extension and the service file has a .img extension. You import partial upgrade files by using either the
system import
command, the webUI, or by logging in as a root user and using SCP to copy files to the system at the
images/import/os
or
images/import/services
directories respectively.
Patch upgrade
Upgrades or patches a subset of Base OS software. The patch file has a
                        .patch extension. You import patch upgrade files by using either the
file import
command, the webUI, or by logging in as a root user and using SCP to copy
                        files to the system at the
images/import/services
directory.
Note:
Currently, the patch upgrade option is not supported on
                            F5OS software. However, this option might be supported for all versions

results.title: K000152446: Intermittent Packet Loss and Latency on BIG-IP LTM
results.content:
# Article K000152446

1.  
Description
Intermittent packet loss and latency may occur on BIG-IP LTM systems running as F5OS tenants, when peer device has
a MAC address which is just for receiving traffic but not used for sending traffic to F5, particularly in environments where
VRRP is enabled. 
With VRRP enabled on peer device, BIG-IP learnd gateway MAC address via ARP and it's VRRP virtual MAC adddress.
BIG-IP use this MAC as destination when sending traffic out. But this virtual MAC address is never used for sending
traffic to BIG-IP except for ARP response.
So FDB entry for this MAC will not be refreshed via business traffic. When FDB entry for this virtual expires, BIG-IP have
to reply on new ARP request to learn it. During the learning process, if the outgoing traffic is huge, packet loss might be
seen.
Symptoms include TCP retransmissions, delayed traffic, and missing packets between the tenant and F5OS or from
F5OS to backend servers.  
Environment
VRRP
Platform: R5000 R10000 rSeries platforms or VELOS platform with FPGA chip.
Peer network device has MAC address just for receiving traffic but not sending traffic 
Cause
F5OS does not dynamically learn the VRRP virtual MAC address when the VRRP device uses its system MAC as the
source MAC for outgoing packets while using the virtual VRRP MAC as destination. As a result:
The business traffic does not update or refresh the dynamic FDB entry for the virtual VRRP virtual MAC.
When this entry expires (default timeout: 5 minu

results.title: Authentication & Access
results.content:
Manual Chapter
:
Authentication & Access
Applies To:
Show
Versions
F5OS-C
1.8.0
Authentication & Access
Authentication and access overview
You can manage the
VELOS
system
at all levels
from the CLI, the webUI, or using REST APIs.
Each of these levels is distinct from one another requiring separate user names and passwords.
The
VELOS
system has
three
levels of user management:
System controller level (chassis)
At the system controller level, after basic configuration is complete, the system includes default root (Bash access only) and admin accounts that you can use to log in to the system. The system controller administrator uses the admin account and changes the default passwords when logging in the first time. At that point, the admin user can also create additional accounts for other users, such as other system controller administrators or operators. The system controller administrator also creates the chassis partitions, terminal server administrators, or operators.
Chassis partition level
At the chassis partition level, the chassis partition administrator logs into the chassis partition previously created by the system controller administrator. The chassis partition provides a default admin account and a chassis partition root account. The chassis partition administrator utilizes the admin account for managing the chassis partition, adding users, such as additional partition administrators, operators, and tenant console operators. You can use the root account to log into

results.title: K000152853: Migration from old BIG-IP to rSeries
results.content:
# Article K000152853

1.  
2.  
3.  
4.  
5.  
6.  
7.  
8.  
Description
Steps to perform a migration from legacy BIG-IP appliances to F5OS-based rSeries platforms using UCS file and the
platform-migrate option.
Environment
Migration from legacy BIG-IP to rSeries using UCS file with the platform-migrate option.
Cause
Migration to rSeries.
Recommended Actions
Upgrade the legacy BIG-IP appliance to a version matching the target tenant. Refer to  forK86001294
compatibility.
Deploy the rSeries appliance as per the following manual: F5 rSeries system initial configuration
Create a BIG-IP tenant with resources sized appropriately for expected traffic load (vCPU, memory, disk) and
assign necessary VLANs and interfaces.
On the source device, generate a UCS file and copy the master key:
tmsh save sys ucs <filename>
f5mku -K
Remove trunk objects from the UCS file before importing it to the target device (see K50152613 )
On the target device (tenant), install the master key that you copied from the source device and load the UCS:
f5mku -r <key_value>
tmsh load sys ucs <filename> no-license platform-migrate
If UCS import fails, review error messages and manually edit configuration files to resolve issues:

The F5 Journeys migration utility is a tool for migrating legacy BIG-IP configurations to BIG-IP tenants running on
F5OS-managed platforms such as VELOS and rSeries.
To improve code security, governance, and release control, F5 moved the F5 Journeys tool from GitHub to the MyF5

migration strategy, including the transition toward  and upcoming migration capabilities.platform-migrate

For  select .Product Line F5 Journeys App
For  select the latest version.Product Version
Complete the remaining steps to download a file to your workstation.

The decision to move F5 Journeys from GitHub to MyF5 Downloads was made to:
Increase code security and integrity.
Ensure controlled distribution.
Align with F5’s software delivery and security policies.
Reduce ambiguity around supported and validated builds.
No functional regression was introduced because of this move

Description
During migration of a BIG-IP configuration from an iSeries system to an rSeries tenant using the F5 BIG-IP Journeys
utility, the “Discover version automatically” (target version) dropdown may not show newer BIG-IP versions, and the
migration may fail when the destination tenant software version is not compatible with the source UCS/TMOS version.
Environment
Source platform: iSeries
Target platform: rSeries (BIG-IP tenant on F5OS)
Tool: F5 BIG-IP Journeys utility
Symptom: Target BIG-IP version missing from the Journeys “Discover version automatically” dropdown
Cause
The Journeys tool’s embedded/pre-defined version list may not include the required BIG-IP software version.
Incompatible version alignment between the source UCS/TMOS and the destination BIG-IP tenant can prevent
migration.
Recommended Actions
Verify Journeys is updated to a current release.
Ensure the destination BIG-IP tenant software version is compatible with the source UCS/TMOS version before
migrating; if needed, provision a tenant on a compatible version and retry.
If the desired BIG-IP version is missing from the Journeys dropdown, select the closest available target version to
complete the migration, then upgrade the tenant afterward to the desired version.
If migration fails, re-check version compatibility be

results.content:
# Article K50152613

Description
Loading UCS from platform/VE BIG-IP  to Tenant with platform-migrate option is failing. /var/log/ltm has logs similar to
shared below
load_config_files[17729]: "/usr/bin/tmsh -n -g -a load sys config partitions all  platform-migrate" -
failed. -- 01070674:3: Trouble allocating a trunk ID for trunk (trunk_object_name).
Unexpected Error: Loading configuration process failed.
 
2025 Jul  2 10:23:30 localhost.localdomain load_config_files[17729]: "/usr/bin/tmsh -n -g -a load sys
config partitions all  platform-migrate" - failed. -- 01070674:3: Trouble allocating a trunk ID for
trunk (trunk_object_name).
Environment
F5OS-A Tenant
UCS from Platform
platform-migrate
Cause
Bug ID 658943
The trunk (called LAG) is created in F5OS-A, not in Tenant, thus Tenant can not allocate ID for the Trunk object.
Recommended Actions
Log in to CLI of the Tenant (or original BIG-IP)
Run the below commands to extract the ucs file to a temporary directory
mkdir /tmp/<foldername>
tar xzvf <full path to your UCS archive> -C /tmp/<foldername>
Edit the bigip_base.conf file to remove the trunk object and any reference to it    
vi /tmp/<foldername>/bigip_base.conf
Then pack up ucs file with the following commands:

results.title: K000160448: Using iHealth to diagnose BIG-IP systems from a qkview
results.content:
# Article K000160448

Description
This article is for you who are trying to do basic troubleshooting/investigation into what is happening on your BIG-IP. In
order to do this you will have to upload a QKView to iHealth.
After generating a QKView file (also known as a "support snapshot" when you generate it in the configuration utility), you
may upload it to ihealth.
WHY SHOULD YOU USE IHEALTH:
For proactive or reactive reasons:
: You want to be familiar with your BIG-IP default behavior and apply recommended practices before an issueProactive
occurs (see the section about Diagnostics further down)
: iHealth is a huge help for troubleshooting most issues, it is an easy way to go through the logs.Reactive
Note: The BIG-IP is not a logging machine, although it enables your to search for logs, there are better options out there
(you can use your favorite search engine and look search for "SIEM").
iHealth is a platform for analyzing your BIG-IP, the resources, configuration and logs.
Note: Logs might be truncated, hence we recommend you to generate the qkview right after the issue occurs (if there is
one), and enable the "Unlimited snaplen" option, that would be the "-s0" flag via the CLI.
For more information on how to generate a QKView file, see "Related Content" section at the bottom of this article.
Environment
BIG-IP, all versions
Cause
None
Answer/Recommended Actions
The iHealth login
The iHealth page can be found , or you can copy paste the URL: HERE https://account.f5.com/i

results.title: K000134457: Overview of F5 VELOS chassis controller services
results.content:
# Article K000134457

Topic
The VELOS system chassis controller services perform a variety of functions, such as configuring and controlling switch
chips, managing partitions and tenants, and performing high availability (HA) failover actions between system controllers.
For a list of the VELOS partition services running at the partition and blade level, refer to K000134684: Overview of F5
.VELOS partition services
Description
The following tables list a description for the various VELOS system services at the system controller level, and the
impact to the VELOS system operation if each service is not running.
Service Name Description Impact if not
running
alert-service
The alert-service runs on both system controllers as well
as each blade installed in the VELOS system. The
installed blades and the standby system controller
forward all their alert messages to the alert service on
the active system controller. The alert service on the
active system controller aggregates all alerts and
publishes them to ConfD.
Container image name: alert_service
No alerts occur.
authentication-mgr
The authentication service manages the configuration
settings for Authentication, Authorization and Accounting
(AAA) at both the chassis and partition levels. F5OS
uses standard Linux PAM modules for local and remote
authentication.
Container image name: authentication-mgr
No authentication
configuration
changes can occur.
The LACP daemon used for the backplane
(management ports) LACP link aggregation

Description
This article provides a comprehensive guide to all available methods for establishing communication between the BIG-IP
system and pool members. It covers configuration steps, use cases, best practices, and relevant KB article references
for each method.
Environment
BIG-IP LTM (All versions)
Classic BIG-IP (versions 9.x - 17.x, 21.x)
F5OS-based systems (rSeries, VELOS)
All deployment scenarios (on-premises, cloud, hybrid)
Cause
The BIG-IP system requires network connectivity to pool members for load balancing and health monitoring. While the
most common method is configuring a self IP on the same subnet as pool members, there are multiple alternative
approaches depending on network architecture, security requirements, and deployment scenarios.
This article covers  for BIG-IP to communicate with pool members:11 different methods
Self IP on the Same Subnet (Most Common)
Default Gateway / Static Routes
Route Domains
SNAT (Source Network Address Translation)
Tunnels (GRE, VXLAN, IPsec)
VLANs with Tagged Interfaces (802.1Q)
Forwarding Virtual Servers
Proxy ARP
Multiple Self IPs on the Same VLAN
Direct Server Return (DSR) / nPath
Service Discovery and Dynamic Pool Members

results.title: Bug ID 659596: bigd not rotating DNS servers when ICMP packet is not received
results.content:
F5 Bug ID 659596: bigd not rotating DNS servers when ICMP packet is not received

Severity: 3-Major | Opened: Apr 20, 2017 | Affected Versions: 13.0.1, 13.0.0, 12.1.3, 12.1.2, 12.1.1, 12.1.0, 12.0.0, 11.6.3, 11.6.2, 11.6.1

Symptoms: When the first DNS server becomes unavailable for FQDN node resolution, but no ICMP message is received, bigd does not rotate to the next DNS server. In the common case where a DNS server becomes unavailable, an ICMP message will notify bigd of server unavailability, and bigd will rotate to the next available DNS server. However, if network configuration fails to route this ICMP message back to bigd, the DNS server is assumed to remain available (and all subsequent DNS resolve operations will fail, as the DNS server is unavailable).

Conditions: -- Multiple DNS servers are configured for FQDN resolution for 'bigd' monitors. -- The first DNS server becomes unavailable. -- ICMP messages are not received by 'bigd'. -- BIG-IP software versions earlier than 12.1.3.3 and 13.1.0.

Impact: bigd continues to send FQDN node resolutions to the (now-unavailable) DNS server, which will fail to resolve (bigd will not rotate to the next configured DNS server). Existing resolved FQDN nodes and all associated monitors will continue to function normally.

Workaround: Ensure network routes ICMP messages to bigd when the DNS server becomes unavailable.

Related articles: K09642539, K10134038

results.content:
F5 Bug ID 749738: After upgrade to 13.1.3.3 or 13.1.3.4, B2250 blades may fail to detect HSB and have restarting chmand

Severity: 1-Blocking | Opened: Nov 14, 2018 | Fixed In: 15.0.0, 13.1.3.5 | Affected Versions: 13.1.3.4, 13.1.3.3

Symptoms: When booting after upgrade, BIG-IP system software may not fully start up, preventing normal service -- In early boot messages in /var/log/ltm: + warning chmand[xxxxx]: 012a0004:4: No FPGA HSB LBB PCI device found. + emerg chmand[xxxxx]: 012a0000:0: Dataplane INOPERABLE - No HSBe2_v2 found on the platform (A112) -- In later ltm logs, chmand may be seen restarting every few seconds with these lines, among others, logged each time it starts: + notice chmand[xxxxx]: 012a0005:5: Starting ChassisManager (chmand). + info chmand[xxxxx]: 012a0006:6: Found platform 'A112' in /PLATFORM. -- The chmand process may create core in /var/core when it restarts.

Conditions: -- Upgrade B2250 (A112) blade to software version 13.1.3.3 or 13.1.3.4. -- This issue occurs on affected BIG-IP versions running directly on the indicated hardware platforms, or running as a vCMP host on the indicated hardware platforms.

Impact: No service after upgrade. Note: This might not occur in every configuration. Some multi-blade configurations might encounter this issue where others do not.

Workaround: If possible, roll back to a previous version and contact F5 Support to get an engineering hotfix containing a fix for this issue.

Fix Information: Upgrading BIG-IP softwar

Symptoms: The readme file automatically produced for BIG-IP Engineering Hotfixes contains the following instructions: This hotfix may not be operational without a FULL system restart. To accomplish this, use the command: /usr/bin/full_box_reboot However, the full_box_reboot command is not part of the documented or recommended workflows for current BIG-IP versions.

Conditions: These instructions are contained in the .readme file that may accompany a BIG-IP Engineering Hotfix provided by F5 to resolve critical issues, under the terms and conditions of the F5 critical issue hotfix policy as described at: https://my.f5.com/manage/s/article/K4918

Impact: The instructions in the Engineering Hotfix readme file may be confusing due to inconsistency with documented workflows for installing BIG-IP Engineering Hotfixes.

Workaround: After the software installs and boots to the volume with installed software no further reboot is required.

results.title: Bug ID 1000061: Elasticsearch process in DCDs restarts every few hours
results.content:
F5 Bug ID 1000061: Elasticsearch process in DCDs restarts every few hours

Severity: 2-Critical | Opened: Mar 07, 2021 | Fixed In: 8.0.0.1

Symptoms: Attempting to aggregate statistics data from BIG-IQ can cause the Elasticsearch (ES) process in the data collection devices (DCDs) to restart every few hours. When this happens, the following exception appears in the elasticsearch/esnode.log file for affected DCDs: java.lang.NoClassDefFoundError: org/elasticsearch/client/RestClient

Conditions: Many statistic dimension combinations are defined for a specific module, which might create more unique ES documents than the allowed document limit per BIG-IP (50000 / DCDs num). For example, a managed BIG-IP device configured with an HTTP module might have traffic from multiple client IPs, with each client IP having multiple destination URLs.

Impact: When BIG-IQ attempts to aggregate the statistics data under these conditions, the ES process fails and then restarts, resulting in the loss of that aggregated statistics data and pending module events.

Workaround: Upgrade F5 Elasticsearch plugins.

Fix Information: This issue is fixed and the Elasticsearch process no longer restarts every few hours.

Related articles: K10134038, K61201515

When the iRules RESOLVER::summarize command is used on a virtual server, undisclosed

results.title: K13614065: Error Message: 010e0999:4: Duplicate oid index found:
results.content:
# Article K13614065

Description
You may get the following error message on a VIPRION platform. For example:
warning snmpd[7592]: 010e0999:4: Duplicate oid index found: bigip_if.c:374
Environment
VIPRION
SNMP
Cause
Duplicate if-index are assigned to the interfaces of non-existing blades, and these old configurations may be left in mcpd
 database, so   process reports a warning for such duplicate OIDs.snmpd
Recommended Actions
The messages are benign and you can safely ignore them.  The duplicate if-index configuration may look like this:
net interface 3/1.1 {
    if-index 0
net interface 3/1.2 {
    if-index 0
You may summarize and sort up all the duplicate if-index. In the following example, there are 8 duplicate interfaces with
if-index value of 0.
tmsh list /net interface all-properties -hidden | grep if-index | sort -n | uniq -c | sort -nr | head 
8     if-index 0               
1     if-index 98
1     if-index 97
 
K13614065: Error Message: 010e0999:4: Duplicate oid index found:
bigip_if.c:374

results.title: K000147633: Why do alerts show WAF security events but none are
results.content:
# Article K000147633

What Happened?
The system is detecting potential security issues that are being caught in the Alert Dashboard (Web and API
) but they are not displayed on the Security Monitor of the HTTP Loadprotections > Default > Notifications > Alerts
Balancer (LB).
Environment
Web and API protection Alerts
Security Monitor
Resolution/Answer
When a system detects something that could be a security problem, it generates a record of that event, called a security
. The system then analyzes these events and generates an alert when it detects a pattern or behavior that couldevent
be a sign of a security issue.
The is a notification that is sent to IT or Security teams to let them know that something is happening that couldalert 
require their attention.
To summarize, a security event is a record of something that might impact the security of a system, and an alert is a
notification sent to IT or Security teams to inform them of potential security issues.
 
Additional Information
Sometimes, an alert might appear even when there's no actual security event. This could be due to a false alarm, a
system error, or overly sensitive settings.
Related Content
Alert Reference
App Security

shown on the Security Dashboard of the HTTP LB?

results.title: K000150252: The meaning of "Select Network on the site" menu
results.content:
# Article K000150252

Description
When you set up Origin Pool on specific sites, there is an option named "Select Network on the site":
 
Environment
F5® Distributed Cloud (SaaS)
F5® Distributed Cloud Load Balancer
CE
SLO/SLI interface
 
Answer
This option allows you to choose CE/site interface that will be in use to forward customer traffic to
origin pool.
K000150252: The meaning of "Select Network on the site" menu
https://my.f5.com/manage/s/article/K000150252
 Published Date: Mar 06, 2025 UTC Updated Date: Dec 07, 2025 UTC

Related Content
 K000147817: What is the difference between SLI and SLO nodes on Two Interface Ingress/Egress
Gateway
https://docs.cloud.f5.com/docs-v2/multi-cloud-network-connect/how-to/site-management
When we select the interface such as inside/outside/segment, it simply tells which interface to use as the egress
interface for the traffic to origin pool and which IP to use as the source IP. This settings does not create any routing
entries. CE/site will NOT add a route entry for the origin pool subnet. 
CE/Site cannot force traffic out of the interface if there is no route and doesn't automatically create routes for origin pool
networks.
To summarize:
we specific CE/site interface in origin pool which is responsible for traffic handling
packets are forwarded to the interface and based on routing table records correlated with that interface, we
forward them
if there is no route to origin pool on selected interface, we drop such packets.

results.content:

RESOLVER::name_lookup
DNSMSG
K52638558: RESOLV::lookup iRule command vulnerability CVE-2016-6876
Description
What command can be used to replace  in iRuleRESOLV::lookup
Environment
LTM
iRule
Software version 15.1.x and above
Cause
None
Recommended Actions
RESOLV::lookup command has been deprecated with the release of version 15.1 in favor of the RESOLVER and
DNSMSG namespaces.
 attempts to send a DNS request using a specified net resolver, a domain name, and any ofRESOLVER::name_lookup
A, AAAA, TXT, MX, PTR, SRV, and NAPTR query types. A DNS request is sent, and response will returned in the form
of an opaque dns_message structure. This structure can be used by  and the  iRuleRESOLVER::summarize DNSMSG
commands to extract the specific data in a user friendly format.
Additional Information
RESOLV::lookup command is still supported in software version 15.x and 16.x. but it is advised to move to new
alternatives.

results.title: Bug ID 655211: bigd crash (SIGSEGV) when running FQDN node monitors
results.content:
F5 Bug ID 655211: bigd crash (SIGSEGV) when running FQDN node monitors

Severity: 2-Critical | Opened: Apr 04, 2017 | Fixed In: 13.1.0, 13.0.1, 12.1.3, 11.6.3.2 | Affected Versions: 11.6.1, 11.6.1 HF1, 11.6.1 HF2, 11.6.2, 11.6.2 HF1, 11.6.3, 11.6.3.1, 12.0.0, 12.0.0 HF1, 12.0.0 HF2, 12.0.0 HF3, 12.0.0 HF4, 12.1.0, 12.1.0 HF1, 12.1.0 HF2, 12.1.1, 12.1.1 HF1, 12.1.1 HF2, 12.1.2, 12.1.2 HF1, 12.1.2 HF2, 13.0.0, 13.0.0 HF1, 13.0.0 HF2, 13.0.0 HF3

Symptoms: bigd processing FQDN node monitors may crash due to a timing issue when processing probe responses.

Conditions: bigd is configured for FQDN node monitors.

Impact: bigd crashes (SIGSEGV). The system restarts bigd automatically, and monitoring resumes. No other action is needed.

Workaround: Although no workaround is available for bigd configured for FQDN node monitors, this crash occurs due to a timing issue, and should be rare.

Fix Information: bigd no longer crashes (SIGSEGV) when running FQDN node monitors due to a timing issue.

results.title: Bug ID 2291161: hud_satellite_process_egress NULL dereference
results.content:
F5 Bug ID 2291161: hud_satellite_process_egress NULL dereference

Severity: 3-Major | Opened: Apr 30, 2026 | Affected Versions: 17.1.0, 17.1.0.1, 17.1.0.2, 17.1.0.3, 17.1.1, 17.1.1.1, 17.1.1.2, 17.1.1.3, 17.1.1.4, 17.1.2, 17.1.2.1, 17.1.2.2, 17.1.3, 17.1.3.1, 17.1.3.2, 17.5.0, 17.5.1, 17.5.1.2, 17.5.1.3, 17.5.1.4, 17.5.1.5, 17.5.1.6, 21.0.0, 21.0.0.1

Symptoms: TMM crashes with a SIGSEGV in hud_satellite_process_egress() when pcb->mate is NULL due to a stale SATELLITE node remaining in the server-side HUD chain after connection reuse.

Conditions: TMM crashes with a SIGSEGV in hud_satellite_process_egress() when pcb->mate is NULL, caused by a stale SATELLITE node remaining in the server-side HUD chain after connection reuse.

Impact: An intermittent TMM crash causes traffic disruption when server-side connections are reused with a stale SATELLITE node in the HUD chain.

Workaround: There is no stable workaround; a partial mitigation involves disabling OneConnect or reducing connection reuse to prevent stale SATELLITE nodes from being carried across connections.

Related articles: K10134038

             Internet Web Replication and Caching Taxonomy

Status of this Memo

   This memo provides information for the Internet community.  It does
   not specify an Internet standard of any kind.  Distribution of this
   memo is unlimited.

Copyright Notice

   Copyright (C) The Internet Society (2001).  All Rights Reserved.

Abstract

   This memo specifies standard terminology and the taxonomy of web
   replication and caching infrastructure as deployed today.  It
   introduces standard concepts, and protocols used today within this
   application domain.  Currently deployed solutions employing these
   technologies are presented to establish a standard taxonomy.  Known
   problems with caching proxies are covered in the document titled
   "Known HTTP Proxy/Caching Problems", and are not part of this
   document.  This document presents open protocols and points to
   published material for each protocol.

   This memo provides information for the Internet community.  It does
   not specify an Internet standard of any kind.  Distribution of this
   memo is unlimited.

Copyright Notice

Abstract

   Content (distribution) internetworking (CDI) is the technology for
   interconnecting content networks, sometimes previously called
   "content peering" or "CDN peering".  A common vocabulary helps the
   process of discussing such interconnection and interoperation.  This
   document introduces content networks and content internetworking, and
   defines elements for such a common vocabulary.

results.content:
Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF)                       W. Hardaker
Request for Comments: 8027                                       USC/ISI
BCP: 207                                                  O. Gudmundsson
Category: Best Current Practice                               CloudFlare
ISSN: 2070-1721                                          S. Krishnaswamy
                                                                 Parsons
                                                           November 2016


                       DNSSEC Roadblock Avoidance

Abstract

   This document describes problems that a Validating DNS resolver,
   stub-resolver, or application might run into within a non-compliant
   infrastructure.  It outlines potential detection and mitigation
   techniques.  The scope of the document is to create a shared approach
   to detect and overcome network issues that a DNSSEC software/system
   may face.

Status of This Memo

   This memo documents an Internet Best Current Practice.

   This document is a product of the Internet Engineering Task Force
   (IETF).  It represents the consensus of the IETF community.  It has
   received public review and has been approved for publication by the
   Internet Engineering Steering Group (IESG).  Further information on
   BCPs is available in Section 2 of RFC 7841.

   and how to provide feedback on it may be obtained at
   http://www.rfc-ed

            Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP/1.1): Caching

Abstract

   The Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) is a stateless application-
   level protocol for distributed, collaborative, hypertext information
   systems.  This document defines HTTP caches and the associated header
   fields that control cache behavior or indicate cacheable response
   messages.

results.content:

                                        University of Rome "Tor Vergata"
                                                              G. Wilkins
                                                                 Webtide
                                                              April 2011


                    Known Issues and Best Practices
    for the Use of Long Polling and Streaming in Bidirectional HTTP

Abstract

   On today's Internet, the Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) is often
   used (some would say abused) to enable asynchronous, "server-
   initiated" communication from a server to a client as well as
   communication from a client to a server.  This document describes
   known issues and best practices related to such "bidirectional HTTP"
   applications, focusing on the two most common mechanisms: HTTP long
   polling and HTTP streaming.

                             HTTP Semantics

Abstract

   The Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) is a stateless application-
   level protocol for distributed, collaborative, hypertext information
   systems.  This document describes the overall architecture of HTTP,
   establishes common terminology, and defines aspects of the protocol
   that are shared by all versions.  In this definition are core
   protocol elements, extensibility mechanisms, and the "http" and
   "https" Uniform Resource Identifier (URI) schemes.

   This document updates RFC 3864 and obsoletes RFCs 2818, 7231, 7232,
   7233, 7235, 7538, 7615, 7694, and portions of 7230.

results.title: Effects of Pervasive Encryption on Operators
results.content:
Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF)                  K. Moriarty, Ed.
Request for Comments: 8404                                      Dell EMC
Category: Informational                                   A. Morton, Ed.
ISSN: 2070-1721                                                AT&T Labs
                                                               July 2018


              Effects of Pervasive Encryption on Operators

Abstract

   Pervasive monitoring attacks on the privacy of Internet users are of
   serious concern to both user and operator communities.  RFC 7258
   discusses the critical need to protect users' privacy when developing
   IETF specifications and also recognizes that making networks
   unmanageable to mitigate pervasive monitoring is not an acceptable
   outcome: an appropriate balance is needed.  This document discusses
   current security and network operations as well as management
   practices that may be impacted by the shift to increased use of
   encryption to help guide protocol development in support of
   manageable and secure networks.
